B113.1.stem: A superficial spreading melanoma is sent for molecular testing. Mutation of which pathway is present (BRAF and NRAS combined) in up to ~80% of cutaneous melanomas AND benign melanocytic nevi, marking it as the central driver of melanocytic neoplasia?

options.A: BRAF or NRAS (MAPK pathway)
options.B: GNAQ or GNA11 (Gq pathway)
options.C: KIT receptor tyrosine kinase
options.D: PTEN loss (PI3K–AKT pathway)

B113.1.exp: The MAPK cascade (RAS→RAF→MEK→ERK) is the master proliferative pathway of melanocytes, and melanoma is fundamentally a disease of its constitutive activation: a mutation in BRAF (a serine/threonine kinase) or in NRAS (a small GTPase upstream of it) is present in up to ~80% of cutaneous melanomas. Crucially, the *same* mutations are found in ordinary benign melanocytic nevi — so MAPK activation alone drives only a proliferative burst that then enters oncogene-induced senescence (a built-in brake), and a nevus stays benign until secondary hits (e.g. CDKN2A or TERT-promoter lesions) release that brake and permit malignant progression. This shared-driver biology is the conceptual core of melanocytic neoplasia and the rationale for BRAF/MEK-inhibitor therapy. The other options are genuine drivers, but each belongs to a *different* melanocytic compartment: GNAQ/GNA11 (Gq-class G-protein α-subunits feeding into PKC/MAPK) define uveal melanoma and blue nevi; KIT (a receptor tyrosine kinase) typifies acral, mucosal, and high-CSD melanoma; and PTEN loss (a lipid phosphatase whose loss disinhibits the PI3K–AKT survival pathway) is a cooperating, not an initiating, event.
B113.1.hy: BRAF/NRAS (MAPK) is mutated in up to 80% of cutaneous melanomas and nevi — the core driver axis.

B113.2.stem: BRAF V600E, the commonest BRAF mutation in melanoma, drives constitutive MAPK signaling through which precise molecular change?

options.A: Substitution of glutamic acid for valine at codon 600
options.B: Substitution of lysine for valine at codon 600
options.C: An in-frame kinase fusion that deletes the autoregulatory domain
options.D: A promoter mutation that creates a new transcription-factor binding site

B113.2.exp: V600E denotes substitution of glutamic acid (E) for valine (V) at codon 600 of the BRAF kinase domain; the negatively charged glutamate mimics the phosphorylation that normally activates the kinase, so it locks BRAF in its active conformation and drives the MAPK cascade independent of upstream RAS or receptor input. Because the lesion is a single point mutation in the ATP-binding kinase domain, it is directly druggable — the structural basis for selective BRAF inhibitors such as vemurafenib and dabrafenib. V600E is by far the commonest BRAF mutation (~90% of BRAF-mutant melanomas) and predominates in low-CSD/superficial-spreading tumors. The distractors are each a real alternative molecular mechanism: V600K (lysine for valine) is a genuine non-E variant enriched in high-CSD/chronically sun-damaged melanoma of older patients; an in-frame kinase fusion that excises the autoregulatory domain is how "pan-negative" (BRAF/NRAS/NF1-wild-type) melanomas activate MAPK; and a promoter mutation that creates a new transcription-factor binding site is the TERT-promoter mechanism — a distinct non-coding lesion that reactivates telomerase rather than a kinase.
B113.2.hy: V600E = glutamic acid→valine at codon 600 → constitutive MAPK; V600K is the high-CSD variant.

B113.3.stem: A melanoma on the sole and a separate sinonasal mucosal melanoma are tested. Mutation/amplification of which gene is most characteristic of acral lentiginous and mucosal melanomas, raising the possibility of a KIT-inhibitor (e.g. imatinib)?

options.A: KIT
options.B: BRAF V600E
options.C: CDKN2A
options.D: GNA11

B113.3.exp: KIT is a type-III receptor tyrosine kinase (the stem-cell-factor receptor, also called CD117) that normally drives melanocyte development; activating point mutations or gene amplification of KIT are found in ~15–40% of acral lentiginous, mucosal, and high-CSD melanomas. These tumors arise on UV-shielded or chronically sun-damaged sites where BRAF is usually wild-type, so KIT becomes the principal MAPK driver — and because it is a kinase already targeted in other cancers, KIT-mutant melanoma can respond to KIT inhibitors such as imatinib (though responses are partial and not durable, and depend on the specific mutation). The distractors anchor to other compartments shown in Table 113.1: BRAF V600E dominates non-CSD/superficial-spreading melanoma of intermittently sun-exposed skin; CDKN2A is the high-penetrance familial-melanoma germline gene that accounts for only ~2% of sporadic tumors; and GNA11 (with GNAQ) marks uveal melanoma and blue nevi, not acral/mucosal tumors.
B113.3.hy: KIT = acral/mucosal/high-CSD melanoma → potential imatinib target.

B113.4.stem: Which statement about GNAQ/GNA11 mutations is correct?

options.A: They occur in 80–90% of uveal melanomas and in blue nevi, yet are absent in cutaneous melanoma
options.B: They are the single most common driver found in high-CSD cutaneous melanomas of the elderly
options.C: They are present in roughly 50–60% of superficial spreading (low-CSD) cutaneous melanomas
options.D: They are the activating mutation that defines Spitz melanomas and atypical Spitz tumors

B113.4.exp: GNAQ and GNA11 encode the α-subunits of Gq-class heterotrimeric G proteins; their activating mutations lock the G protein "on," constitutively signaling through phospholipase-C/PKC and downstream MAPK. These mutations occur in 80–90% of uveal (choroidal) melanomas and in benign blue nevi — the lineage of dermal dendritic melanocytes — but are characteristically ABSENT in conventional epidermal cutaneous melanoma, which is the classic board discriminator (they mark the dermal/ocular melanocytic compartment, not the epidermal one). The wrong options misassign drivers: high-CSD cutaneous melanoma of the elderly is most often NRAS-mutant (with NF1 also common); superficial spreading melanoma is typically BRAF V600E; and Spitz tumors are defined by HRAS mutations or NTRK/ALK/ROS1 kinase fusions, not GNAQ/GNA11.
B113.4.hy: GNAQ/GNA11 = uveal melanoma + blue nevi, not cutaneous melanoma.

B113.5.stem: CDKN2A is the major high-penetrance familial-melanoma gene. Which statement about it is correct?

options.A: It encodes both p16 and p14ARF, and explains roughly 2% of all cutaneous melanomas
options.B: It encodes the BRAF kinase and is somatically mutated in about half of all melanomas
options.C: Its germline loss is the defining lesion underlying the BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome
options.D: It is amplified rather than deleted in the large majority of uveal (choroidal) melanomas

B113.5.exp: CDKN2A is remarkable because a single locus encodes two distinct tumor-suppressor proteins through alternate reading frames: p16^INK4a inhibits CDK4/6, keeping Rb hypophosphorylated to enforce the G1→S cell-cycle checkpoint, while p14ARF inhibits MDM2, thereby stabilizing p53. Loss of CDKN2A therefore cripples BOTH the Rb and the p53 axes at once — disabling two independent guardians of the cell cycle, which is why it is such a potent melanoma-predisposition gene. It is the most common germline mutation in familial (heritable) melanoma kindreds, yet because most melanoma is sporadic it explains only ~2% of all cutaneous melanomas — a denominator trap. The distractors describe other genes: BRAF is the MAPK kinase somatically mutated in roughly half of melanomas; BAP1 (a nuclear deubiquitylase), not CDKN2A, defines the BAP1 tumor-predisposition syndrome; and uveal melanomas are driven by GNAQ/GNA11 (often with BAP1 loss), not by amplification of CDKN2A.
B113.5.hy: CDKN2A → p16 + p14ARF; commonest familial gene but ~2% of sporadic melanoma.

B113.6.stem: Loss of NF1, activating both the MAPK and PI3K pathways, is most characteristically found in which melanoma context?

options.A: Desmoplastic melanoma (~25%) and high-CSD melanoma (~30%)
options.B: Uveal melanoma (~50%) and melanoma arising in blue nevi
options.C: Acral lentiginous and mucosal melanoma (~15–40%)
options.D: Spitz melanoma and atypical Spitz tumors of childhood

B113.6.exp: NF1 (neurofibromin) is a RAS-GAP — a GTPase-activating protein that switches RAS *off* by accelerating its GTP hydrolysis; losing NF1 therefore leaves RAS chronically GTP-bound and active, simultaneously firing both the MAPK and the PI3K–AKT pathways. This makes NF1 a third recurrent MAPK driver class alongside BRAF and NRAS, and it is characteristically enriched in desmoplastic melanoma (~25%, where its high mutational burden also reflects heavy UV damage) and in high-CSD melanoma (~30%). NF1-mutant tumors are part of the "triple-wild-type/pan-negative" group when BRAF and NRAS are absent. The distractors place the wrong driver at each site: uveal melanoma is GNAQ/GNA11-driven (with BAP1 loss in roughly half, marking metastatic risk); acral and mucosal melanoma favor KIT and NRAS; and Spitz tumors carry HRAS mutations or kinase fusions.
B113.6.hy: NF1 loss = desmoplastic (~25%) and high-CSD (~30%) melanoma.

B113.7.stem: A germline E318K variant in MITF is associated with which phenotype?

options.A: Light skin, high nevus count, and increased melanoma and renal cell carcinoma risk
options.B: Café-au-lait macules, axillary freckling, neurofibromas, and optic glioma risk
options.C: Numerous early basal cell carcinomas with odontogenic jaw cysts and palmar pits
options.D: Marked photosensitivity and early skin cancers from defective nucleotide-excision repair

B113.7.exp: MITF (microphthalmia-associated transcription factor) is the master regulator of the melanocyte lineage, driving pigment genes and survival/proliferation. The germline p.E318K variant disrupts a SUMOylation site on MITF, removing a normal post-translational brake so the transcription factor is hyperactive — producing a phenotype of light skin and a high nevus count with an increased risk of melanoma (including amelanotic forms) and, distinctively, renal cell carcinoma (a melanoma–RCC predisposition allele). It is a moderate-penetrance susceptibility gene rather than a high-penetrance Mendelian one. Each distractor is a separate, well-defined cancer-predisposition phenotype: café-au-lait macules with axillary/inguinal freckling, neurofibromas, and optic-glioma risk is neurofibromatosis type 1; numerous early basal cell carcinomas with odontogenic jaw cysts and palmar pits is Gorlin (nevoid BCC) syndrome from PTCH1 loss; and marked photosensitivity with early skin cancers from defective nucleotide-excision repair is xeroderma pigmentosum.
B113.7.hy: MITF E318K = light skin, ↑nevi, melanoma + RCC risk.

B113.8.stem: A "pan-negative" melanoma (wild-type BRAF, NRAS, NF1) is found to carry an NTRK gene fusion. Which agent specifically targets this alteration?

options.A: Larotrectinib, a selective TRK inhibitor
options.B: Vemurafenib, a selective BRAF V600 inhibitor
options.C: Imatinib, a KIT/ABL receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor
options.D: Trametinib, an allosteric downstream MEK pathway inhibitor

B113.8.exp: A subset of "pan-negative" melanomas (wild-type for BRAF, NRAS, and NF1) instead activate MAPK through in-frame kinase fusions — NTRK1/2/3, ALK, or ROS1 fusions — in which a partner gene replaces the kinase's autoregulatory domain, leaving it constitutively on. An NTRK fusion is therefore a true oncogenic driver and is directly actionable with the selective TRK inhibitor larotrectinib (a "tumor-agnostic" approval). Matching the drug to the exact lesion is the point of the item, and each distractor needs a *different* molecular target: vemurafenib is a selective BRAF V600 inhibitor and is useless without a V600 mutation; imatinib targets the KIT receptor of acral/mucosal melanoma; and trametinib is a MEK inhibitor given *with* a BRAF inhibitor to block MAPK downstream, not a stand-alone match for an NTRK fusion.

B113.9.stem: The 2018/2023 WHO classification of melanoma is organized primarily around which axis?

options.A: Cumulative sun damage (high-, low-, non-CSD) plus site of origin
options.B: The Breslow thickness category as measured at the time of diagnosis
options.C: The presence or absence of histologic ulceration of the primary tumor
options.D: Predominance of the radial versus the vertical growth phase histologically

B113.9.exp: The 2018/2023 WHO classification of melanocytic neoplasia is organized around the *pathway of evolution* — primarily the degree of cumulative sun damage (high-CSD, low-CSD, or non-CSD/UV-shielded) integrated with anatomic site of origin and the characteristic mutation pattern at each (e.g. BRAF V600E in low-CSD, NRAS/NF1/V600K in high-CSD, KIT in acral/mucosal). This framework deliberately links biology to anatomy and replaces the older purely morphologic subtype list, because it predicts the likely driver mutation and hence therapy. The distractors are all genuine, important variables — Breslow thickness and ulceration are the determinants of AJCC T-stage and prognosis, and the distinction between a radial (horizontal, intraepidermal) and a vertical (dermal-invasive, metastasis-competent) growth phase is a key histologic concept — but none of them is the organizing axis of the WHO molecular-anatomic classification.
B113.9.hy: WHO melanoma classes = cumulative sun damage + site + mutation pattern.

B113.10.stem: Tyrosinase, gp100/PMEL17, and MART-1/Melan-A belong to which class of melanoma antigen?

options.A: Melanocyte differentiation (lineage) antigens
options.B: Cancer/testis shared tumor-specific antigens
options.C: Mutant tumor-specific neoantigens (e.g. p16)
options.D: Viral oncoproteins from an oncogenic virus

B113.10.exp: Tyrosinase, gp100/PMEL17, and MART-1/Melan-A are melanocyte *differentiation* (lineage) antigens — normal melanosomal/pigment-synthesis proteins that are overexpressed by melanoma cells and presented on MHC, so autologous cytotoxic T cells can recognize them; this is why they double as both immunotherapy/vaccine targets and diagnostic immunohistochemical markers. They are "self" antigens, which is why targeting them can also cause autoimmune vitiligo. The distractors are the other antigen categories the chapter enumerates: cancer/testis antigens (e.g. MAGE-1/-3, NY-ESO-1) are normally silenced except in germline tissue and re-expressed by tumors; mutant tumor-specific neoantigens arise from somatic mutations such as altered p16/CDKN2A and are truly tumor-restricted; and viral oncoproteins drive a *different* skin cancer — Merkel-cell carcinoma (Merkel-cell polyomavirus) — not conventional melanoma.

B113.11.stem: CTLA-4, the target of ipilimumab, inhibits T-cell activation by which mechanism?

options.A: An inhibitory CD28 homolog that competes for B7, removing costimulation
options.B: A receptor whose ligand PD-L1 triggers apoptosis of tumor-specific T cells
options.C: An inhibitory receptor blocked by the anti-LAG3 antibody relatlimab
options.D: A molecule presenting antigen on MHC class I to cytotoxic T cells

B113.11.exp: T-cell activation requires two signals: antigen via the TCR, plus costimulation when CD28 on the T cell binds B7 (CD80/86) on the antigen-presenting cell. CTLA-4 is an inhibitory homolog of CD28 that is upregulated *after* the T cell activates and binds B7 with much higher affinity, outcompeting CD28 and thereby stripping away the costimulatory signal — a built-in negative-feedback brake that limits the early (priming/afferent) phase of the response, predominantly in the lymph node. Ipilimumab, an anti-CTLA-4 antibody, releases this brake and was the first checkpoint inhibitor approved. The distractors describe sibling mechanisms: the PD-1/PD-L1 axis drives apoptosis of tumor-specific T cells at the later effector phase in the tumor itself; LAG3 is a separate inhibitory receptor (the relatlimab target); and presenting antigen on MHC class I is the job of the tumor cell/APC, not a function of CTLA-4.
B113.11.hy: CTLA-4 = CD28 homolog that steals B7 → removes costimulation (ipilimumab target).

B113.12.stem: During checkpoint-inhibitor therapy, development of vitiligo-like leukoderma is notable because it:

options.A: Correlates with improved progression-free survival
options.B: Mandates immediate permanent discontinuation of immunotherapy
options.C: Signals progression to distant metastatic disease
options.D: Predicts treatment failure and a switch to chemotherapy

B113.12.exp: Vitiligo-like leukoderma during checkpoint-inhibitor therapy is a melanocyte-directed immune-related adverse event: the same T-cell response unleashed against melanoma differentiation antigens (tyrosinase, MART-1, gp100) also attacks normal melanocytes, producing depigmentation. Occurring in roughly 2–25% of treated patients, it is a *marker of an active, effective antitumor immune response*, and its appearance correlates with improved progression-free (and overall) survival — so it is a favorable on-treatment sign, not a reason to stop therapy. It is typically asymptomatic and managed expectantly. The distractors invert this biology: it does not mandate discontinuation, does not signal metastatic progression, and does not predict treatment failure — quite the opposite.
B113.12.hy: Checkpoint-inhibitor vitiligo = good prognostic sign (better PFS).

B113.13.stem: Which is a recognized mechanism of melanoma immune escape?

options.A: Loss of MHC class I molecules from the tumor-cell surface
options.B: Upregulation of MHC class II on all surrounding keratinocytes
options.C: Increased CD28-mediated costimulation of cytotoxic T cells
options.D: Enhanced presentation of tumor neoantigens to dendritic cells

B113.13.exp: Melanoma evades the immune system by becoming invisible and inhospitable to T cells. Loss of MHC class I from the tumor-cell surface removes the platform on which cytotoxic CD8 T cells recognize tumor peptides, so even a primed T cell cannot "see" the cell — a core escape mechanism listed in Table 113.2, alongside loss/downregulation of the tumor antigens themselves, secretion of immunosuppressive cytokines (IL-10, TGF-β), and upregulation of inhibitory ligands (PD-L1, CTLA-4 pathway). Each of these tilts the microenvironment toward tolerance. The distractors are all immune-*promoting* and therefore the opposite of escape: more MHC class II presentation, more CD28-mediated costimulation, and enhanced neoantigen presentation to dendritic cells would each strengthen, not weaken, antitumor immunity.
B113.13.hy: Immune escape = lose MHC I/antigens, secrete IL-10/TGF-β, upregulate PD-L1/CTLA-4.

B113.14.stem: Immune checkpoint blockade was first introduced clinically for melanoma. Which agent was approved first?

options.A: Ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4)
options.B: Nivolumab (anti-PD-1)
options.C: Pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1)
options.D: Relatlimab (anti-LAG3)

B113.14.exp: Ipilimumab, an anti-CTLA-4 antibody, was the first immune checkpoint inhibitor ever approved, and melanoma was the disease in which checkpoint blockade was first proven — a landmark because it was the first therapy to show a durable overall-survival benefit in metastatic melanoma. The class then expanded in order: the anti-PD-1 antibodies nivolumab and pembrolizumab followed and became the preferred first-line monotherapy (better tolerated, often combined with ipilimumab for high-risk disease), and the anti-LAG3 antibody relatlimab (paired with nivolumab) is the most recent addition. The distractors — nivolumab, pembrolizumab, relatlimab — are all genuine checkpoint agents, but each reached approval *after* ipilimumab, so the discriminator is recalling that CTLA-4 blockade came first.
B113.14.hy: Checkpoint blockade debuted in melanoma with ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4).

B113.15.stem: The PD-1/PD-L1 interaction promotes tumor immune escape by:

options.A: Driving apoptosis of tumor-specific T cells while sparing regulatory T cells
options.B: Competing with CD28 for B7 to strip away the early costimulatory signal
options.C: Blocking the loading of antigenic peptides onto tumor-cell MHC class I
options.D: Preventing antigen-loaded dendritic cells from migrating to the draining node

B113.15.exp: PD-1 is an inhibitory receptor on activated T cells; when tumor (or stromal) PD-L1/PD-L2 engages it, the resulting signal drives apoptosis of the tumor-antigen-specific *effector* T cells while sparing — even favoring the survival of — regulatory T cells, tilting the local microenvironment toward tolerance. Unlike CTLA-4, which acts early during priming in the node, the PD-1 axis operates at the later effector phase *within the tumor*, which is why anti-PD-1 antibodies (nivolumab, pembrolizumab) reactivate exhausted tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes. The distractors belong to other checkpoints/escape routes: competing with CD28 for B7 to remove costimulation is the CTLA-4 mechanism, while blocking MHC-class-I peptide loading or preventing dendritic-cell migration to the draining node are distinct (afferent-phase) ways tumors hide, not the PD-1 mechanism.
B113.15.hy: PD-1/PD-L1 = apoptosis of tumor-specific T cells, sparing Tregs (effector-phase brake).

B113.16.stem: The annual increase in cutaneous melanoma incidence in White populations has generally been estimated at:

options.A: 3–7% per year, predicting a doubling every 10–20 years
options.B: Under 1% per year, with essentially stable long-term rates
options.C: About 15% per year, the fastest rate of any human cancer
options.D: About 25% per year, doubling roughly every 3 years

B113.16.exp: Across White populations the annual rise in cutaneous-melanoma incidence has been roughly 3–7%, a rate that compounds to a doubling every 10–20 years and has made melanoma one of the most rapidly increasing cancers in fair-skinned populations. The increase is partly real (more intense intermittent UV and tanning-bed exposure) and partly an artifact of heightened detection of thin tumors — a nuance that explains why mortality has not climbed in parallel with incidence. Knowing the order of magnitude matters because it frames the public-health rationale for sun protection and screening. The distractors are the neighbouring wrong magnitudes used as traps: a stable/<1% rate understates the well-documented climb, while ~15–25% per year (doubling every ~3 years) wildly overstates it.
B113.16.hy: Melanoma incidence rising ~3–7%/yr → doubling every 10–20 yr.

B113.17.stem: Which region carries the world's highest cutaneous melanoma incidence rates?

options.A: Australia/New Zealand (~54 per 100,000 per year)
options.B: Northern Europe (~25–26 per 100,000 per year)
options.C: North America (high but below Australasia)
options.D: Southern Europe (lower than Northern Europe)

B113.17.exp: Australia and New Zealand carry the world's highest cutaneous-melanoma incidence (~54 per 100,000 per year) — the product of a predominantly fair-skinned, Northern-European-descended population living at low latitudes under intense ambient UV with an outdoor lifestyle. This combination of a susceptible phenotype and a high UV dose is exactly the gene–environment interaction that maximizes melanoma risk. Northern Europe is the next-highest band (~25–26 per 100,000), reflecting the same fair phenotype but far less UV. The other listed regions — North America (high but below Australasia) and Southern Europe (lower than Northern Europe, as the more pigmented Mediterranean phenotype offsets the sunnier climate) — have substantial but lower rates, so the discriminator is simply recalling that Australia/New Zealand leads the world.

B113.18.stem: Which statement about melanoma mortality and tumor-thickness trends is correct?

options.A: Mortality has stabilized/declined since the 1990s, though older men still show rising rates
options.B: Mortality has fallen uniformly across every age group and both sexes in all countries worldwide
options.C: Mean Breslow thickness at diagnosis has risen steadily across recent decades in most populations
options.D: The proportion of thick melanomas at diagnosis falls progressively with each increase in patient age

B113.18.exp: Melanoma incidence and mortality have diverged: since the 1990s mortality has broadly stabilized or even declined while incidence keeps climbing — a pattern driven chiefly by earlier detection of thin, curable tumors and, more recently, by genuinely effective systemic therapy (checkpoint and targeted agents). The improvement is *not* uniform, however: older men remain a group with persistently rising mortality, partly because they present later with thicker tumors. This is the correct, nuanced statement. The distractors are plausible-sounding inversions of the real trends: the decline is not uniform across all ages and sexes (older men rise); mean Breslow thickness at diagnosis has *fallen*, not risen, with earlier detection; and the proportion of thick melanomas *increases* with patient age (reaching roughly 20% by age 80), rather than falling.
B113.18.hy: Mortality stabilizing (thinner tumors), but older men still rising; thick-tumor share ↑ with age.

B113.19.stem: A patient has more than 100 common melanocytic nevi. Approximately what relative risk of melanoma does this confer?

options.A: ~8- to 10-fold
options.B: ~2-fold
options.C: ~3- to 4-fold
options.D: ~20- to 30-fold

B113.19.exp: Having >100 common melanocytic nevi confers a relative melanoma risk of roughly 8–10-fold, making total nevus count one of the strongest *phenotypic* (non-genetic) risk markers we have. It is powerful because a high nevus count integrates two things: cumulative UV exposure and an inherited melanocytic-proliferation phenotype, and the dose–response is near-linear (each additional band of nevi adds risk). This is also why total-body photography and self-examination are emphasized in high-nevus patients. The distractors are the adjacent risk magnitudes for *other* lesion categories listed in Table 113.3 — multiple solar lentigines confer roughly 3–4×, while atypical (dysplastic) nevi reach much higher (≥5 atypical ~4–6×, up to ~32× at the extreme) — so the reader must match the right lesion type to the right number rather than recall a single figure.
B113.19.hy: >100 common nevi ≈ RR 8–10×; the dose-response is near-linear.

B113.20.stem: Above how many clinically atypical melanocytic nevi does relative risk rise sharply (defining atypical mole syndrome), with little further increase beyond that threshold?

options.A: 5 or more
options.B: 2 or more
options.C: 10 or more
options.D: 50 or more

B113.20.exp: Five or more clinically atypical (dysplastic) melanocytic nevi marks a sharp step-up in melanoma risk and is the threshold used to define atypical mole syndrome — typically present alongside a high common-nevus count (≥50, often >100). The biology is that atypical nevi are both direct (rare) precursors and, more importantly, *markers* of a melanoma-prone phenotype with abundant active melanocytes; beyond about five atypical nevi the studies show relatively little *further* incremental rise, so five is the operational cut-point. The distractors are the adjacent counts that do not define this threshold: 2 atypical nevi is too few to define the syndrome; 10 is where the maximum reported risk (~32×) is seen rather than the syndrome threshold; and 50 refers to the *common*-nevus count, a different marker.
B113.20.hy: ≥5 atypical nevi = atypical mole syndrome threshold; risk plateaus beyond.

B113.21.stem: Which phenotype reflects the pheomelanin-predominant, high-risk pigmentation pattern?

options.A: Red hair and blue eyes, a tendency to burn, and an inability to tan
options.B: Black hair and brown eyes that tan readily without ever burning
options.C: Extensive congenital dermal melanocytosis over the trunk and buttocks
options.D: Constitutively dark (Fitzpatrick phototype V–VI) sun-resistant skin

B113.21.exp: Red hair, blue eyes, and a tendency to burn without tanning reflects a *pheomelanin*-predominant pigmentation phenotype, classically driven by loss-of-function MC1R variants. Melanocytes make two pigments: brown-black eumelanin, which absorbs and shields against UV, and red-yellow pheomelanin, which gives little photoprotection and can itself generate reactive oxygen species under UV (pro-oxidant) — so a pheomelanin-shifted person sustains more UV and oxidative DNA damage, the basis of the elevated melanoma risk. This is among the strongest constitutional risk phenotypes. The distractors are lower-risk patterns: a dark-haired, brown-eyed phenotype that tans readily without burning has efficient eumelanin protection; congenital dermal melanocytosis is a benign deep-dermal pigment finding; and constitutively dark (Fitzpatrick V–VI) skin carries the lowest melanoma risk of all.

B113.22.stem: Regarding ultraviolet exposure and melanoma, which statement is correct?

options.A: Indoor tanning before age 35 substantially raises risk and is a classified human carcinogen
options.B: Intense intermittent sun exposure lowers melanoma risk relative to chronic daily exposure
options.C: Blistering childhood sunburns bear essentially no relationship to later melanoma risk
options.D: Residence at equatorial latitudes is protective through gradual pigment adaptation

B113.22.exp: Indoor (artificial-UV) tanning substantially raises melanoma risk, and the effect is greatest when exposure begins young — first use before age 35 carries the highest excess risk because early UV damage to dividing melanocytes has the longest latency to act. On this evidence the WHO/IARC classifies tanning devices as a Group 1 human carcinogen, the basis for legislation restricting minors' access. The distractors are factual reversals of established UV epidemiology: intense *intermittent* recreational sun exposure (and blistering childhood sunburns) *raises* melanoma risk rather than lowering it; sunburns — especially in childhood — are firmly linked to later melanoma; and residence at equatorial/low latitudes *increases* UV dose and risk in susceptible skin rather than being protective.
B113.22.hy: Tanning beds <35 = carcinogen, ↑melanoma; intermittent sun & childhood sunburns also raise risk.

B113.23.stem: Which population has increased melanoma risk specifically because of acquired immunosuppression?

options.A: Solid-organ transplant recipients and people living with HIV infection
options.B: Patients using topical retinoids long-term for chronic inflammatory acne
options.C: Patients with active, spreading, non-segmental generalized vitiligo
options.D: Otherwise healthy women taking combined oral contraceptive pills

B113.23.exp: Acquired immunosuppression raises melanoma risk by weakening immune surveillance against transformed melanocytes — the same T-cell response that checkpoint inhibitors exploit therapeutically. The two paradigmatic groups are solid-organ transplant recipients on chronic immunosuppressants and people living with HIV; both show increased melanoma incidence and often worse outcomes, underscoring that intact immunity normally restrains the disease. The distractors are non-risks or even inverse associations: long-term topical retinoids for acne are not a melanoma risk factor; vitiligo, far from raising risk, actually correlates with a *better* response to checkpoint immunotherapy; and combined oral contraceptives have not been shown to increase melanoma risk.
B113.23.hy: Transplant recipients and HIV = immunosuppression-related ↑melanoma risk.

B113.24.stem: Why is prophylactic removal of clinically banal melanocytic nevi NOT recommended?

options.A: Single-nevus transformation is ~1/thousands (common) to 1/hundreds (atypical), and most melanoma is de novo
options.B: Banal melanocytic nevi essentially never carry any of the driver mutations that are also seen in melanoma
options.C: Excising a melanocytic nevus paradoxically raises the risk of a melanoma developing later at distant sites
options.D: Melanomas that arise within a pre-existing benign nevus are uniformly fatal regardless of complete excision

B113.24.exp: Prophylactic excision of clinically banal nevi is not recommended because the arithmetic does not support it: any single common nevus has a transformation risk on the order of 1 in thousands (1 in hundreds for an atypical nevus), and most melanomas arise *de novo* in normal-appearing skin rather than within a pre-existing nevus — so removing benign moles would entail enormous numbers of excisions to prevent very few cancers, while doing nothing about the de-novo majority. The correct strategy is surveillance and excision only of clinically suspicious or changing lesions. The distractors are each false: banal nevi *can* and frequently do carry BRAF and other melanoma driver mutations (which is exactly why driver presence alone does not equal malignancy); excising a nevus does not raise the risk of melanoma at distant sites; and melanomas that do arise within a nevus are not uniformly fatal — outcome depends on stage, as for any melanoma.
B113.24.hy: Single-nevus transformation ≈ 1/thousands–1/hundreds; most melanoma is de novo → don't excise banal nevi.

B113.25.stem: Approximately what proportion of familial melanoma kindreds carry a germline CDKN2A mutation?

options.B: About 2%
options.D: About 5%

B113.25.exp: Germline CDKN2A mutations are detected in roughly 20–40% of familial melanoma kindreds (the reported range spans about 5–70% by geographic region and how stringently "familial" is defined). CDKN2A is the major high-penetrance familial-melanoma gene, but even within selected high-risk families it accounts for only a minority — which is why multi-gene panels and counseling are still needed when CDKN2A is negative. The classic trap is the adjacent figure ~2%, which is a *different denominator*: that is the fraction of *all cutaneous melanomas* (the mostly sporadic total) attributable to CDKN2A, not the proportion of familial kindreds carrying it. The other options (70–90%, ~5%) are simply the wrong magnitudes for kindred-level carriage.
B113.25.hy: CDKN2A in 20–40% of familial kindreds, but only ~2% of all cutaneous melanomas.

B113.26.stem: Which statement about MC1R variants and melanoma is correct?

options.A: They are low/moderate-penetrance pigment alleles; two variants raise BRAF-mutant melanoma risk up to ~17×
options.B: They constitute the single highest-penetrance familial melanoma susceptibility gene currently known
options.C: They encode the p16 tumor-suppressor protein that brakes the CDK4/6–Rb cell-cycle checkpoint
options.D: They are somatic UV-induced mutations rather than inherited germline pigmentation gene variants

B113.26.exp: MC1R encodes the melanocortin-1 receptor, the switch that, when activated, shifts melanocytes from making pheomelanin to protective eumelanin; common loss-of-function MC1R variants are inherited, low-to-moderate-penetrance pigmentation alleles (the "red-hair-color" variants). They raise overall melanoma risk only modestly (~1.5–3×), but the striking finding is a gene–gene interaction: carrying two MC1R variants raises the risk of *BRAF-mutant* melanoma specifically by up to ~17-fold — pigment genotype amplifying a somatic-driver-defined subset. The distractors misclassify MC1R: the highest-penetrance familial melanoma gene is CDKN2A, not MC1R; the p16 tumor-suppressor protein is encoded by CDKN2A (not MC1R); and MC1R variants are inherited germline polymorphisms, not somatic UV-induced mutations.
B113.26.hy: MC1R = pigmentation susceptibility allele; 2 variants → up to 17× BRAF-mutant melanoma.

B113.27.stem: A patient develops several small red-orange papules ("BAPomas"/MBAITs) showing loss of nuclear BAP1 staining. The associated tumor syndrome predisposes to which constellation?

options.A: Cutaneous and uveal melanoma, mesothelioma, and renal cell carcinoma
options.B: Pancreatic carcinoma, astrocytoma, and early-onset cutaneous melanoma
options.C: Medullary thyroid carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, and mucosal neuromas
options.D: Basal cell carcinomas with palmar pits, jaw cysts, and medulloblastoma

B113.27.exp: BAP1 (BRCA1-associated protein-1) is a nuclear deubiquitylase and tumor suppressor; germline loss-of-function causes the BAP1 tumor-predisposition syndrome, which predisposes to cutaneous *and* uveal melanoma, mesothelioma, and renal cell carcinoma (with lesser basal cell carcinoma, meningioma, and cholangiocarcinoma risk). Its hallmark skin lesions are BAP1-inactivated melanocytomas — variously called MBAITs or "BAPomas" — small red-orange papules whose diagnosis rests on loss of nuclear BAP1 staining on immunohistochemistry, mirroring the germline second hit. Recognizing the skin clue lets the dermatologist flag a kindred at risk for lethal internal tumors. The distractors are other tumor syndromes: pancreatic carcinoma plus neural tumors with early melanoma fits CDKN2A (familial melanoma–pancreatic cancer syndrome); medullary thyroid carcinoma with pheochromocytoma and mucosal neuromas is MEN2; and basal cell carcinomas with palmar pits, jaw cysts, and medulloblastoma is Gorlin (nevoid BCC) syndrome.
B113.27.hy: BAP1 syndrome = cutaneous + uveal melanoma, mesothelioma, RCC; MBAITs lose nuclear BAP1.

B113.28.stem: By the AAD "rule of 3," genetic testing for familial melanoma is indicated for an individual with:

options.A: ≥3 primary invasive melanomas, or a family with ≥3 melanoma/pancreatic-cancer diagnoses on one side
options.B: A single thin invasive melanoma in a person who has no relevant personal or family history of cancer
options.C: Any patient over the age of 40 years who presents with their first single primary invasive melanoma
options.D: Only those patients in whom a pathogenic germline CDKN2A mutation has already been formally confirmed

B113.28.exp: The AAD "rule of 3" is a practical filter that identifies the individuals most likely to carry a CDKN2A (or related) germline mutation and therefore most likely to benefit from genetic testing/counseling: a *personal* history of ≥3 primary invasive melanomas, OR a *family* with ≥3 combined invasive-melanoma and/or pancreatic-cancer diagnoses among close relatives on the same side of the family (reflecting the CDKN2A melanoma–pancreatic-cancer link). The threshold concentrates testing where the pre-test probability of a high-penetrance mutation is meaningful, avoiding low-yield testing of sporadic cases. The distractors fall below this bar: a single thin melanoma in a person with no relevant history, or simply being over age 40 with a first melanoma, does not meet criteria; and an already-confirmed pathogenic mutation is the *result* of testing, not a prerequisite for it.
B113.28.hy: Rule of 3 = ≥3 melanomas in one person, or ≥3 melanoma/pancreatic dx on one side of the family.

B113.29.stem: For genetic risk assessment of familial melanoma, current AAD guidance prefers:

options.A: Multi-gene panel testing rather than single-gene (CDKN2A-only) testing
options.B: Single-gene CDKN2A testing alone as sufficient in essentially all cases
options.C: Routine germline testing of every patient who presents with one melanoma
options.D: Deferring counseling entirely unless a pathogenic mutation is already known

B113.29.exp: Current AAD guidance prefers multi-gene panel testing over CDKN2A-only testing for familial-melanoma risk assessment, because melanoma predisposition is genetically heterogeneous: a CDKN2A-negative kindred may still carry mutations in CDK4, BAP1, MITF, POT1, or other susceptibility genes, several of which also raise the risk of *internal* malignancies (e.g. pancreatic cancer, mesothelioma, RCC) that change surveillance. A panel captures these in one assay and avoids the false reassurance of a negative single-gene test. The distractors over- or under-reach: germline testing is *not* recommended for every patient with a single melanoma (only for those meeting criteria such as the rule of 3); single-gene CDKN2A testing alone is no longer the preferred approach; and genetic counseling is recommended for appropriate patients regardless of whether a mutation has already been identified.
B113.29.hy: Use multi-gene panels (not CDKN2A alone) for familial-melanoma genetic testing.

B113.30.stem: The Nambour (Queensland) randomized trial provided the pivotal evidence that regular sunscreen use:

options.A: Reduced new primary melanoma incidence by ~50–73% at 10-year follow-up
options.B: Had no effect on melanoma but reduced actinic keratosis counts only
options.C: Increased melanoma risk by lowering circulating vitamin D levels
options.D: Lowered melanoma mortality but left overall incidence unchanged

B113.30.exp: The Nambour Skin Cancer Prevention trial (Queensland, Australia) is the pivotal randomized controlled trial — the highest level of evidence — showing that sunscreen prevents melanoma, not merely keratinocyte cancers: regular daily application of SPF-16 sunscreen reduced new primary cutaneous melanoma incidence by roughly 50–73% when measured about 10 years after the intervention period. Because melanoma develops slowly, the benefit only became apparent at long follow-up, which is why this trial is so often cited as the definitive sun-protection evidence. The distractors understate or invert the result: the trial demonstrated a genuine reduction in *melanoma* (not an actinic-keratosis-only effect); it did not increase risk through lowered vitamin D; and its headline endpoint was melanoma *incidence*, not mortality.
B113.30.hy: Nambour RCT: daily sunscreen ↓ new melanoma ~50–73% at 10 yr.

B113.31.stem: Which is the most common subtype of cutaneous melanoma in light-skinned individuals, accounting for ~60–70% of cases and arising most often in the fourth-to-sixth decades?

options.A: Superficial spreading melanoma
options.B: Nodular melanoma
options.C: Lentigo maligna melanoma
options.D: Acral lentiginous melanoma

B113.31.exp: Superficial spreading melanoma (SSM) — broadly corresponding to "low-CSD" (low cumulative-sun-damage) melanoma, the WHO molecular pathway driven by intermittent sun exposure and BRAF mutation in younger skin — is the commonest subtype in light-skinned individuals (~60–70%), diagnosed most often in the fourth-to-sixth decades. The defining biology is a prolonged radial (horizontal) growth phase: melanocytes spread laterally within and just beneath the epidermis as a slowly enlarging, flat, irregularly pigmented macule for months to years before any vertical (dermal-invasive) phase develops, which is why SSM is often caught thin and curable. Histologically this radial phase shows pagetoid (upward) scatter of atypical melanocytes across the epidermis. The frequency makes it the natural "default" answer for a typical truncal melanoma in a White patient, and recognizing the radial-then-vertical sequence is the key concept that separates it from the others. The same-class distractors are the other classic subtypes with distinct epidemiology and growth biology: nodular melanoma (~10–25%, sixth decade, de novo vertical growth with no radial phase), lentigo maligna melanoma (~10–15%, seventh decade, high-CSD sun-damaged face), and acral lentiginous melanoma (~2–5%, palms/soles/nail, not UV-driven).
B113.31.hy: SSM = most common (~60–70%), low-CSD, radial then vertical growth.
B113.31.src: Bologna Ch 113 · Types of Primary Melanomas, Table 113.5

B113.32.stem: Which statement about superficial spreading melanoma (SSM) is correct?

options.A: It favors the back in men and the legs in women, arising in a pre-existing nevus in about half of cases
options.B: It arises de novo as a vertical-growth-phase tumor without any preceding horizontal radial growth phase
options.C: It occurs almost exclusively on the chronically sun-damaged facial skin of much older elderly patients
options.D: It is the melanoma subtype most disproportionately frequent in darkly pigmented non-White populations

B113.32.exp: SSM shows a sex-specific site distribution that reflects intermittent recreational sun exposure: it favors the trunk (especially the back) in men and the legs in women, the body areas burned during sunbathing. It is also the subtype most likely to arise within a pre-existing melanocytic nevus — roughly half in some series — although the absolute transformation risk of any single nevus remains tiny, so prophylactic removal of ordinary nevi is not justified; the practical point is that a *changing* nevus on the trunk/legs warrants attention. This nevus-association ties to the low-CSD/BRAF pathway, in which acquired nevi and SSM share the BRAF V600E driver. The same-class distractors describe the other subtypes: arising de novo as pure vertical growth (no nevus, no radial phase) is nodular melanoma; chronically sun-damaged facial skin of the elderly is lentigo maligna melanoma (high-CSD); and a disproportionate share of melanomas in darker-skinned patients is acral lentiginous melanoma.
B113.32.hy: SSM: back (men)/legs (women); ~50% arise in a pre-existing nevus.

B113.33.stem: Which feature distinguishes nodular melanoma (NM) from the other growth-pattern subtypes?

options.A: It arises de novo as a vertical-growth-phase tumor with no preceding radial growth phase
options.B: It is defined by a prolonged radial growth phase confined for years to the epidermis
options.C: It is the subtype most strongly correlated with high cumulative ultraviolet sun damage
options.D: It almost always retains heavy uniform pigmentation and very rarely ulcerates or bleeds

B113.33.exp: Nodular melanoma (NM) is distinguished from every other growth-pattern subtype by arising essentially de novo as a vertical-growth-phase tumor — invasive dermal proliferation from the outset — without the prolonged intraepidermal radial growth phase that characterizes SSM, LMM, and ALM. Because there is little or no "in situ" lead-time, NM presents as a rapidly enlarging, often symmetric, dome-shaped papule or nodule and is therefore detected late and thick; the radial phase is what normally gives clinicians the ABCDE window, and NM skips it. This is also why NM contributes disproportionately to thick melanomas and to melanoma deaths despite being less common. The same-class distractors invert these facts: a prolonged epidermal radial phase describes SSM/LMM; cumulative sun damage and a facial location characterize LMM (high-CSD); and the claim that NM cannot ulcerate/bleed or be amelanotic is false — NM commonly ulcerates, bleeds, and can be pigment-poor (hence the EFG rule).
B113.33.hy: NM = de novo vertical growth, no radial phase → presents thick.

B113.34.stem: Although nodular melanoma represents only ~10–25% of melanomas, why does it contribute disproportionately to melanoma deaths?

options.A: It is detected late and thick — making up ~50% of melanomas >2 mm and ~40% of fatal cases
options.B: It is intrinsically more resistant to systemic chemotherapy than other subtypes at equal stage
options.C: Its melanoma-specific survival is far worse stage-for-stage than that of the other subtypes
options.D: It spreads hematogenously to viscera while the other subtypes metastasize only via lymphatics

B113.34.exp: Nodular melanoma's outsized contribution to mortality is fundamentally a *detection* problem rather than a uniquely lethal cell biology: because it lacks the intraepidermal radial growth phase, it grows vertically (into the dermis, the route to metastasis) from the start and is found late and thick — accounting for nearly half of melanomas thicker than 2 mm at diagnosis and roughly 40% of ultimately fatal cases. Breslow thickness is the dominant survival driver, and NM simply presents on the wrong side of that curve. Stage-for-stage (i.e. matched for thickness, ulceration and nodal status), NM survival is not clearly worse than other subtypes — the lesson is that earlier detection, not different chemotherapy, is what would change its outcomes. The distractors propose biology the chapter does not support: there is no demonstrated stage-for-stage survival disadvantage, no special intrinsic chemoresistance, and no exclusively hematogenous (bypassing lymphatics) spread pattern unique to NM.
B113.34.hy: NM kills via late/thick detection (~40% of fatal cases), not worse stage-for-stage biology.

B113.35.stem: Lentigo maligna melanoma (LMM, high-CSD melanoma) characteristically:

options.A: Arises on sun-damaged face/scalp of older adults from a macular in-situ precursor (lentigo maligna)
options.B: Arises on the trunk of young adults who have had intense intermittent recreational sun exposure
options.C: Occurs on the palms, soles, and nail units largely independent of ultraviolet sun exposure
options.D: Presents abruptly as a rapidly growing amelanotic dermal nodule over a few weeks to months

B113.35.exp: Lentigo maligna melanoma (LMM) is the "high-CSD" (high cumulative-sun-damage) subtype — the WHO pathway driven by chronic, year-round UV exposure rather than intermittent burns — and therefore develops on the most photodamaged skin: classically the face, and the bald scalp/ears of older men. Its natural history is slow: it begins as lentigo maligna (LM), an in-situ macular precursor that enlarges horizontally over years as a tan-brown irregular patch, and only later does an invasive (vertical-phase) papule or nodule supervene, at which point it is termed LMM. The background skin shows marked solar elastosis, and the proliferation is lentiginous (single atypical melanocytes along the basal layer) with extension down adnexal/follicular epithelium — features that make margins hard to judge clinically. The same-class distractors describe other subtypes: the trunk of younger adults with intermittent sun is SSM (low-CSD); acral/nail sites independent of UV are ALM; and a rapidly growing pigment-poor nodule is nodular melanoma.
B113.35.hy: LMM = sun-damaged face/scalp of elderly; macular LM precursor.

B113.36.stem: Regarding lentigo maligna (LM), which statement is correct?

options.A: LM is melanoma in situ of sun-damaged skin; about 5% of LM lesions progress to invasive melanoma
options.B: LM is by definition an invasive melanoma as soon as it becomes clinically pigmented and visible
options.C: LM is a wholly benign lentigo of photodamaged skin with essentially no malignant potential at all
options.D: Roughly half of all LM lesions progress to frank invasive melanoma within the first year of onset

B113.36.exp: Lentigo maligna (LM) is melanoma *in situ* — atypical melanocytes confined to the epidermis (no breach of the basement membrane) — arising in chronically sun-damaged skin, and it is the precursor to invasive lentigo maligna melanoma. The estimated lifetime progression to invasive disease is on the order of ~5%, but the true rate is genuinely uncertain because LM is often excised once recognized and because it is hard to date its onset. Two clinical consequences follow: LM is a true malignancy (not a benign lentigo) that warrants definitive treatment, yet its slow, low-percentage progression supports less-aggressive options (e.g. topical imiquimod or radiotherapy) when surgery is impractical in elderly facial sites. The distractors misstate its nature: LM is in situ, not invasive by definition; it is not a benign lesion without malignant potential; and a ~50%-within-a-year progression figure grossly overstates the rate.
B113.36.hy: LM = melanoma in situ of CSD skin; ~5% progress to invasive.

B113.37.stem: Which statement about acral lentiginous melanoma (ALM) is correct?

options.A: Its absolute incidence is similar across races, but it is a far higher proportion of melanomas in Black/Asian patients
options.B: It is caused by cumulative ultraviolet sun exposure delivered directly to the palms and soles over decades
options.C: It is the single most common melanoma subtype overall among non-Hispanic White patients in the SEER data
options.D: It is biologically and genetically identical to superficial spreading melanoma despite its acral location

B113.37.exp: The pivotal epidemiologic point about acral lentiginous melanoma (ALM) is that its *absolute* incidence is roughly similar across racial/ethnic groups, because ALM arises on UV-protected glabrous skin (palms, soles, nail unit) and is not driven by sun exposure. What differs is the *relative* proportion: darker-skinned populations develop few UV-related (SSM/LMM) melanomas, so ALM makes up a disproportionate share of their melanomas — up to ~36% in Black and ~22% in Asian patients, versus only ~2% in non-Hispanic Whites. This is a classic exam trap, because it is tempting (but wrong) to conclude that ALM is biologically "more aggressive in" or "more common in" dark skin in an absolute sense. ALM is also genetically distinct, frequently harboring KIT or cyclin-pathway alterations rather than the BRAF mutations of low-CSD SSM. The distractors are false on each count: ALM is not UV-driven, it is not the commonest subtype in White patients (SSM is), and it is not molecularly identical to SSM.
B113.37.hy: ALM: similar absolute incidence across races, but up to ~36% of melanomas in Black patients.
B113.37.src: Bologna Ch 113 · Acral Lentiginous Melanoma, Table 113.5

B113.38.stem: ALM is frequently diagnosed at an advanced stage. Which factor most contributes to this delay?

options.A: Primary misdiagnosis (e.g. as a wart or traumatic lesion) occurs in roughly one-third of patients
options.B: ALM grows faster than any other melanoma subtype, outpacing the interval between skin examinations
options.C: ALM is essentially invisible to dermoscopy because of the thick keratin of the acral stratum corneum
options.D: ALM never produces any pigment, so it is uniformly amelanotic and missed on routine inspection

B113.38.exp: The dominant reason ALM is diagnosed late is misrecognition, not fast biology: roughly one-third of ALMs are initially mistaken for benign acral conditions — a plantar wart, a callus, a fungal or traumatic nail change, or a subungual hematoma — so the lesion is treated topically or simply observed while it thickens. Two factors compound this: low clinician and patient awareness that melanoma occurs on palms, soles and nails (especially in darker skin), and a high threshold to biopsy acral sites because of pain, slow healing and weight-bearing morbidity. The practical teaching point is to biopsy persistent acral pigmented or non-healing lesions and to use dermoscopy, where the *parallel-ridge pattern* (pigment along the raised ridges/sweat-duct lines of acral skin) flags malignancy whereas a parallel-furrow pattern suggests a benign acral nevus. The distractors are unsupported: ALM is not the fastest-growing subtype, dermoscopy genuinely helps via these acral patterns, and ALM is usually pigmented rather than uniformly amelanotic.
B113.38.hy: ~1/3 of ALMs are misdiagnosed → diagnostic delay and advanced stage.

B113.39.stem: By the SEER frequency data, which ordering of the four classic subtypes from most to least common (overall) is correct?

options.A: Superficial spreading > nodular ≈ lentigo maligna > acral lentiginous
options.B: Nodular > superficial spreading > acral lentiginous > lentigo maligna
options.C: Lentigo maligna > superficial spreading > nodular > acral lentiginous
options.D: Acral lentiginous > nodular > lentigo maligna > superficial spreading

B113.39.exp: Per SEER frequency data (Table 113.5), the descending order of the four classic clinicopathologic subtypes is superficial spreading (~68%) > nodular (~16%) ≈ lentigo maligna (~14%) > acral lentiginous (~2%), and the accompanying median ages rise in parallel (SSM ~54 yr, NM ~61, LMM ~69), reflecting that the sun-damage-dependent subtypes appear in older skin. Two anchors fix the ranking: SSM dominates by a wide margin in light-skinned populations (the low-CSD/BRAF intermittent-sun pathway), and ALM is the *rarest overall* despite being the most prominent subtype in darker-skinned patients — an absolute-versus-relative distinction. NM and LMM are close in overall frequency, so the order hinges on getting SSM first and ALM last. The distractors simply scramble this sequence; recognizing the two endpoints (SSM highest, ALM lowest) is enough to eliminate them.

B113.40.stem: A spindle-cell dermal melanoma stains negative for HMB45, MART-1/Melan-A, and MITF. Which marker is most reliably positive and key to the diagnosis?

options.A: S100 and SOX10
options.B: HMB45 (gp100)
options.C: MART-1/Melan-A
options.D: Cytokeratin AE1/AE3

B113.40.exp: Desmoplastic melanoma is a spindle-cell tumor embedded in a fibrous (desmoplastic) stroma, and its defining diagnostic trap is that the standard melanocyte-differentiation markers — HMB45 (recognizes the melanosomal glycoprotein PMEL17/gp100), MART-1/Melan-A, tyrosinase, and the transcription factor MITF — are frequently *negative* because the spindled cells are poorly differentiated and contain little melanosomal antigen. The markers that rescue the diagnosis are the lineage transcription factors and S100 family: S100 (a calcium-binding protein, highly sensitive for melanocytes including spindled/desmoplastic forms) and especially SOX10, a neural-crest nuclear transcription factor that reliably labels the spindled component with crisp nuclear staining and less background than S100. So when a dermal spindle-cell lesion is HMB45/MART-1/MITF-negative, a positive S100/SOX10 confirms melanocytic origin and clinches desmoplastic melanoma. The distractors are precisely the markers that *fail* in this tumor (HMB45, MART-1) or an epithelial marker (cytokeratin) that would be negative in any melanoma and instead points to carcinoma.
B113.40.hy: Desmoplastic melanoma: HMB45/MART-1/MITF often negative → rely on S100/SOX10.

B113.41.stem: Which set of features is characteristic of desmoplastic melanoma?

options.A: Spindle cells in fibroblastic stroma, frequent neurotropism, NF1 mutation, and possible adjuvant radiotherapy
options.B: Pagetoid epidermal spread of large pigmented epithelioid melanocytes with abundant cytoplasmic melanin
options.C: A dermal dendritic-melanocyte proliferation driven by GNAQ/GNA11, arising as a blue nodule on the scalp
options.D: A KIT-mutant amelanotic tumor of the oral, nasopharyngeal, and anogenital mucosal surfaces

B113.41.exp: Desmoplastic melanoma is defined histologically by spindled melanocytes dispersed in a collagenous (desmoplastic) stroma, and several features cluster together: it shows frequent neurotropism (perineural invasion, tracking along nerves), it carries a very high UV-mutation burden and loss-of-function NF1 mutations in about a quarter of cases (NF1 is a RAS-GTPase-activating tumor suppressor, so its loss activates RAS signalling), and pathologists subclassify it as "pure" (≥90% desmoplastic) versus "mixed." These features drive management: thick lesions with pure desmoplastic histology, extensive neurotropism, or a head/neck location are at high risk of local recurrence and may be considered for adjuvant radiotherapy, and pure DM has a notably lower sentinel-node positivity rate than conventional melanoma. The same-class distractors describe other melanoma entities: pagetoid epithelioid intraepidermal spread is SSM; a GNAQ/GNA11-driven blue-black dermal scalp tumor is blue nevus-like melanoma; and a KIT-mutant mucosal-junction tumor is mucosal melanoma.
B113.41.hy: Desmoplastic = spindle cells + neurotropism + NF1; consider adjuvant RT.

B113.42.section: Melanoma Arising in a Blue Nevus
B113.42.stem: Blue nevus-like melanoma ("malignant blue nevus") most characteristically:

options.A: Is a blue-black scalp dermal nodule with an associated cellular blue nevus and GNAQ/GNA11 (± BAP1) mutations
options.B: Is an indolent intraepidermal lentiginous melanocytic proliferation arising on the palms and soles
options.C: Carries a BRAF V600E mutation and behaves like a low-CSD superficial spreading melanoma of the trunk
options.D: Is the single most common subtype of cutaneous melanoma encountered in prepubertal children

B113.42.exp: Blue nevus-like melanoma ("malignant blue nevus") is a rare dermal melanoma that arises in or resembles a (often cellular) blue nevus, presents most characteristically as a blue-black deeply situated nodule on the scalp, and shares the molecular pathway of blue nevi: the lesion carries GNAQ or (less often) GNA11 mutations — these encode Gq-class G-protein alpha subunits that constitutively activate downstream MAPK/YAP signalling — with additional BAP1, SF3B1, or EIF1AX alterations marking the malignant transformation. The blue-black color is the Tyndall effect of melanin sited deep in the dermis. Clinically it behaves aggressively, with high rates of local recurrence and metastasis, so the deep-seated bland-looking nodule belies its danger. The distractors are the wrong subtypes/biology: an indolent acral intraepidermal proliferation describes acral lentiginous in-situ disease, a BRAF V600E truncal lesion is SSM (low-CSD), and "most common pediatric melanoma" is simply false for this rare entity.
B113.42.hy: Blue nevus-like melanoma = scalp dermal nodule, GNAQ/GNA11 (+BAP1/SF3B1), aggressive.
B113.42.src: Bologna Ch 113 · Melanoma Arising in a Blue Nevus

B113.43.stem: "Primary dermal melanoma" is distinguished from metastatic melanoma chiefly because it:

options.A: Lacks epidermal involvement/regression yet has a favorable prognosis (~80–100% 5-yr) despite thick depth
options.B: Always displays a well-developed overlying intraepidermal in-situ component above the dermal tumor
options.C: Pursues a uniformly fatal metastatic course despite its small size and limited Breslow thickness
options.D: Is defined by a Breslow depth under 1 mm with brisk regression and a prominent radial growth phase

B113.43.exp: Primary dermal melanoma is a melanoma confined to the dermis (and sometimes subcutis) with *no* overlying epidermal (in-situ) component and *no* regression, so on biopsy it looks exactly like a cutaneous metastasis from an occult primary — that mimicry is the whole diagnostic problem. The diagnosis is therefore one of exclusion: it is accepted as a primary only after clinical and radiologic work-up finds no other melanoma to be the source. Despite a deceptively thick measured Breslow depth (often ~3–9 mm), it paradoxically carries a relatively favorable prognosis (~80–100% 5-year survival), much better than a true thick conventional melanoma or true metastatic disease — possibly because it is a single localized clone rather than disseminated tumor. The distractors are false: it characteristically *lacks* an in-situ (epidermal) component (the very feature that, if present, would prove a primary), it is not uniformly fatal, and it is not defined by thin (<1 mm) depth — it is usually thick.
B113.43.hy: Primary dermal melanoma: no epidermal component, thick but favorable (80–100% 5-yr).

B113.44.stem: A uveal (choroidal) melanoma is analyzed for prognosis. Which molecular finding most strongly predicts poor survival?

options.A: Monosomy 3 (loss of a copy of chromosome 3)
options.B: A BRAF V600E activating point mutation
options.C: A germline CDKN2A (p16) gene deletion
options.D: An NTRK1/3 receptor kinase gene fusion

B113.44.exp: Uveal (choroidal) melanoma is prognosticated by cytogenetics, and monosomy 3 (loss of one entire copy of chromosome 3, which carries the BAP1 tumor-suppressor) is the dominant adverse marker: it drops 5-year survival from roughly 100% to under 50%, and the risk is worst when combined with gains of chromosome 8q. This reflects uveal melanoma's strong, almost exclusively *hematogenous* tropism to the liver, so chromosome status is used to triage metastatic surveillance. The driver mutations here are GNAQ/GNA11 (Gq-class G-proteins), not the BRAF/CDKN2A pathway of cutaneous disease, which is why those cutaneous markers do not prognosticate eye tumors. The distractors therefore do not apply to uveal prognostication: BRAF V600E and CDKN2A loss belong to cutaneous melanoma, and NTRK gene fusions characterize Spitz tumors.

B113.45.stem: Which statement about uveal melanoma is correct?

options.A: It harbors GNAQ/GNA11 mutations, metastasizes to the liver, and is treated with proton-beam radiotherapy
options.B: It responds to immune checkpoint inhibitors essentially as well as cutaneous melanoma of similar stage
options.C: It is driven by BRAF V600E and is therefore treated first-line with combined BRAF and MEK inhibitors
options.D: Its single most frequent site of distant metastasis is the lung rather than the liver or the brain

B113.45.exp: Uveal melanoma is biologically distinct from cutaneous melanoma on essentially every axis: it is driven by GNAQ/GNA11 (Gq-class G-protein) mutations rather than BRAF, it metastasizes preferentially and often late to the liver via the bloodstream, and modern management favors eye-sparing proton-beam (or plaque brachytherapy) radiotherapy over enucleation when feasible. Because it carries a low mutational burden and few neoantigens, it responds poorly to the immune-checkpoint inhibitors that revolutionized cutaneous melanoma; the agent specifically approved for metastatic uveal melanoma is tebentafusp, a bispecific molecule that engages CD3+ T cells against gp100, and only in HLA-A*02:01-positive patients. The distractors invert these facts: checkpoint inhibitors do *not* work as well here, it is not BRAF-driven, and its dominant metastatic site is the liver, not the lung.
B113.45.hy: Uveal melanoma = GNAQ/GNA11, liver mets, proton beam; poor ICI response.

options.A: Is ~1% of melanomas, is amelanotic in up to ~35%, and can carry activating KIT mutations
options.B: Is the most common melanoma subtype encountered in fair-skinned White populations
options.C: Is reliably detected at an early stage because of its accessible mucosal location
options.D: Is defined by a BRAF V600E mutation and carries an excellent overall prognosis

B113.46.exp: Mucosal melanomas are rare (~1% of all melanomas) and arise on non-sun-exposed mucosal surfaces — head/neck (sinonasal, oral), anorectal, and vulvovaginal regions, typically near mucocutaneous junctions. Diagnosis is delayed because these sites are hidden and because up to ~35% are amelanotic (pigment-poor), so they masquerade as polyps, hemorrhoids, or benign mucosal lesions and are usually advanced (often nodal/visceral) by the time they are found — hence a generally poor prognosis. Molecularly, like acral lentiginous melanoma, mucosal melanoma is UV-independent and is enriched for activating KIT mutations (and amplifications), which raises the possibility of targeted therapy with KIT inhibitors such as imatinib, though clinical benefit has been limited. The distractors are false on each count: mucosal melanoma is uncommon (not the commonest subtype), is usually advanced rather than caught early, and is not defined by BRAF V600E or by a favorable outcome.
B113.46.hy: Mucosal melanoma: ~1%, ~35% amelanotic, KIT-mutant, poor prognosis.

B113.47.stem: A pregnant woman has a suspicious pigmented lesion. Which management statement is correct?

options.A: Biopsy/excision with lidocaine-epinephrine are safe; if SLNB is needed defer to ≥2nd trimester and avoid blue dyes
options.B: All diagnostic biopsies and definitive excisions must be deferred entirely until after the delivery
options.C: Sentinel-node biopsy should be performed immediately in the first trimester using isosulfan blue dye
options.D: Pregnancy is an absolute contraindication to any surgical treatment of a suspected melanoma at all

B113.47.exp: A suspicious pigmented lesion in pregnancy must be worked up like any other — diagnostic biopsy and definitive wide local excision under local anesthesia (small amounts of lidocaine ± epinephrine are considered safe) should *not* be delayed, because deferring care risks tumor progression that is far more dangerous to mother and fetus than the minor procedures. When sentinel lymph node biopsy is indicated, guidelines advise deferring it until at least the second trimester to minimize the fetal risk of general anesthesia, and the vital-blue dyes (isosulfan blue, methylene blue) are avoided — methylene blue is teratogenic/risky — so a radiotracer (technetium) is used instead, or nodal status is followed by ultrasound. The governing principle is that maternal cancer outcomes drive management; pregnancy modifies the *technique* (anesthesia timing, dye choice), not the decision to treat. The distractors err in opposite directions: deferring all surgery to after delivery under-treats, while first-trimester SLNB with blue dye is unsafe.
B113.47.hy: Pregnancy: biopsy/WLE safe; SLNB ≥2nd trimester; avoid blue dyes.

B113.48.stem: Which statement about hormones, pregnancy, and melanoma is correct?

options.A: Exogenous hormones have not been shown to raise risk, and higher parity is linked to lower melanoma risk
options.B: Combined oral contraceptive pills roughly double a woman's lifetime risk of developing melanoma
options.C: Pregnancy occurring after a melanoma diagnosis markedly increases the risk of disease recurrence
options.D: Postmenopausal hormone replacement therapy is the single strongest known melanoma risk factor

B113.48.exp: Despite the long-held intuition that melanocytes are hormonally responsive, the epidemiologic evidence does not support hormones as a melanoma driver: exogenous hormones — combined oral contraceptives, postmenopausal hormone-replacement therapy, and fertility treatments — have not been shown to increase melanoma incidence, and higher parity (more pregnancies) is actually associated with a modestly *decreased* risk. Pregnancy at the time of diagnosis has likewise not been shown to worsen prognosis in localized/regional (stage 0–III) disease when matched for stage. The clinical message is reassurance: a melanoma history does not, by itself, forbid contraception, HRT, or future pregnancy, and counseling should be individualized rather than fear-based. The distractors invert the data: OCPs do not double the risk, a subsequent pregnancy does not markedly raise recurrence, and HRT is not a major risk factor.
B113.48.hy: Hormones/OCP/HRT not shown to raise melanoma risk; higher parity → lower risk.

B113.49.stem: Because conventional ABCDE criteria often fail in prepubertal children, modified pediatric criteria were proposed. What does the pediatric "ABCD" stand for?

options.A: Amelanotic; Bleeding/Bump; Color uniformity; De novo/any Diameter
options.B: Asymmetry; Border irregularity; Color variation; Diameter >6 mm
options.C: Age young; Brisk mitoses; Caliber of vessels; Depth of invasion
options.D: Atypia; Breslow thickness; Clark level; Dermoscopy pattern

B113.49.exp: Pediatric (especially prepubertal) melanomas frequently violate the adult ABCDE rule — they are often amelanotic, symmetric, well-bordered, uniform in color, and smaller than 6 mm, more closely resembling a wart, pyogenic granuloma, or Spitz nevus than a classic adult melanoma — so a modified pediatric "ABCD" was proposed to flag them. It stands for Amelanotic; Bleeding or Bump (a raised/bleeding lesion); Color uniformity (uniform color, the *opposite* of the adult "color variegation" cue); and De novo appearance / any Diameter (size is not reassuring in children). The point is that relying on adult criteria in a child causes missed diagnoses, so a different pattern of suspicion is needed. The distractors are the adult ABCDE (which uses Diameter >6 mm and color variegation) or invented mnemonics ("Age/Brisk/Caliber/Depth"; "Atypia/Breslow/Clark/Dermoscopy") that do not exist.
B113.49.hy: Pediatric ABCD = Amelanotic, Bleeding/Bump, Color uniformity, De novo/any Diameter.

B113.50.stem: In the ABCDE rule for melanoma, what does "D" denote per Bologna, and which letter do patients most often use to recognize melanoma?

options.A: Diameter >5 mm; "E" (evolution/change) is most often the patient-recognized clue
options.B: Depth beyond 1 mm; "A" (asymmetry) is most often the patient-recognized clue
options.C: Dermoscopy positivity; "C" (color variegation) is the main patient-recognized clue
options.D: Diameter beyond 10 mm; "B" (border irregularity) is the main patient clue

B113.50.exp: In Bologna's ABCDE public-awareness rule, the letters are Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variegation, Diameter >5 mm, and Evolution — note the "D" is *diameter* (a size threshold of >5 mm in this text), not depth or dermoscopy. The clinically pivotal letter is "E": studies show patients most often recognize a melanoma because the lesion *changed* — it is new, or it grew, darkened, or began to itch/bleed — so evolution over time is the single most useful warning sign for self-detection and is the only dynamic criterion in the list. The other letters describe a single snapshot, whereas a benign nevus that suddenly changes is what prompts presentation. The distractors substitute the wrong meaning of "D" (depth, dermoscopy, or a >10 mm cutoff) or name the wrong patient-recognized letter.
B113.50.hy: ABCDE: D = diameter >5 mm; "E" (change) is the key patient clue.

B113.51.stem: A clinician notices one pigmented lesion on the back that looks strikingly different from all the patient's other "signature" nevi. This recognition principle is termed:

options.A: The "ugly duckling" sign
options.B: The Hutchinson sign
options.C: The "Little Red Riding Hood" sign
options.D: The "EFG" rule

B113.51.exp: The "ugly duckling" sign is the principle that, because most of a given patient's nevi share a family-resemblance "signature" pattern, the melanoma is often the one lesion that looks *different from the others* — a comparative, intra-patient outlier — rather than the one that scores worst on absolute ABCDE criteria. This matters because a person with many atypical nevi may have several lesions that each look mildly "abnormal," so the discriminating move is to find the deviant, not to apply a fixed checklist to each; the sign is reproducible even among non-experts and complements ABCDE. The vignette's lesion "strikingly different from all the patient's other nevi" is the textbook trigger. The distractors are different named signs: the Hutchinson sign is periungual pigment spread in subungual melanoma; the "Little Red Riding Hood" sign refers to surrounding erythema/inflammation; and "EFG" (Elevated, Firm, Growing) aids detection of amelanotic nodular melanoma.
B113.51.hy: Ugly duckling = the outlier lesion unlike the patient's other nevi.

B113.52.stem: The "EFG" rule is a clinical aid most useful for detecting which melanoma presentation?

options.A: Amelanotic nodular melanoma — Elevated, Firm, and Growing
options.B: Lentigo maligna on the cheek of a sun-damaged elderly man
options.C: Subungual acral melanoma of the thumb with a pigment band
options.D: Superficial spreading melanoma in situ on the upper back

B113.52.exp: The "EFG" rule — Elevated, Firm, Growing — was devised precisely for the melanoma that defeats the pigment-based ABCDE rule: the amelanotic nodular melanoma. Because that tumor has little or no pigment, it shows neither color variegation nor an irregular pigment border, so it is missed by ABCDE and by dermoscopy's pigment cues; the EFG triad instead keys on the physical behavior of a vertical-growth nodule (a firm, raised lesion that is enlarging), prompting biopsy of a fast-growing pink/flesh nodule even without pigment. This complements ABCDE rather than replacing it, covering the most lethal and most easily overlooked presentation. The distractors are presentations where pigment-based or other signs already apply: lentigo maligna melanoma (ABCDE/dermoscopy on the face), subungual melanoma (the Hutchinson sign), and SSM in situ (ABCDE on a flat pigmented macule).

B113.53.stem: Clinical (naked-eye) diagnostic accuracy for melanoma and the effect of dermoscopy are best described as:

options.A: ~75% by visual inspection alone, rising to ~90% with expert dermoscopy use
options.B: ~95% by visual inspection alone, with expert dermoscopy adding essentially nothing
options.C: ~50% by visual inspection alone, left entirely unchanged by the use of dermoscopy
options.D: ~30% by visual inspection alone, paradoxically falling further when dermoscopy is used

B113.53.exp: Unaided (naked-eye) clinical diagnosis of melanoma is correct in at best around ~75% of cases, but in trained hands dermoscopy (epiluminescence microscopy, which visualizes subsurface pigment and vascular structures invisible to the naked eye) raises diagnostic accuracy to roughly ~90% and simultaneously reduces unnecessary biopsies of benign lesions by improving specificity. The crucial caveat is that dermoscopy is not infallible: an estimated 10–25% of melanomas are "featureless" — they lack the specific dermoscopic criteria — so a clinically changing or "ugly duckling" lesion should still be biopsied even if dermoscopy looks bland. The take-home is that dermoscopy is a powerful adjunct used by trained clinicians, not a replacement for clinical judgment or histopathology. The distractors either over- or under-state these accuracy figures or wrongly claim dermoscopy is useless or harmful.

B113.54.stem: In the two-step dermoscopic algorithm, step 1 asks whether a lesion is melanocytic. Which finding qualifies a lesion as melanocytic?

options.A: A pigment network, aggregated globules, streaks, homogeneous blue, or an acral parallel pattern
options.B: Arborizing (branched) telangiectasias radiating across an otherwise pearly translucent papule
options.C: Multiple milia-like cysts together with comedo-like openings in a "stuck-on" verrucous plaque
options.D: Sharply demarcated red-blue lacunae corresponding to dilated vascular spaces in the dermis

B113.54.exp: The two-step dermoscopic algorithm first asks "is this lesion melanocytic?" before any melanoma-specific scoring, because the malignancy criteria only apply to melanocytic lesions. A lesion is classified as melanocytic in step 1 if it shows at least one positive melanocytic clue: a pigment network (reticular pigmented lines), aggregated brown globules, radial streaks/pseudopods, diffuse homogeneous blue pigment, or — on glabrous acral skin — a parallel (furrow/ridge) pattern. Only after a lesion is deemed melanocytic does step 2 grade it for melanoma features; mislabeling a basal cell carcinoma or seborrheic keratosis as melanocytic would misroute the whole analysis. The distractors are exactly the criteria that mark *non-melanocytic* tumors and thus *exclude* melanocytic origin: arborizing vessels (basal cell carcinoma), milia-like cysts and comedo-like openings (seborrheic keratosis), and red-blue lacunae (vascular lesion/hemangioma).
B113.54.hy: Melanocytic clues = network, globules, streaks, homogeneous blue, acral parallel pattern.

B113.55.stem: Which local dermoscopic feature most specifically raises concern for melanoma?

options.A: A blue-white veil
options.B: Comma-shaped vessels
options.C: A regular narrow network
options.D: Multiple milia-like cysts

B113.55.exp: The blue-white veil is a confluent, irregular gray-blue to whitish-blue structureless zone, and it is among the most *specific* local dermoscopic criteria for melanoma — its histologic correlate is an acanthotic epidermis with compact orthokeratosis/hypergranulosis (the white component) overlying heavily pigmented melanocytes and melanin deep in the dermis (the blue component, via the Tyndall effect). Because it reflects deep pigment plus epidermal change, it usually signals an invasive (vertical-phase) lesion, which is why it carries such high specificity even though it is not present in every melanoma. The distractors point to benign or non-melanoma diagnoses: comma-shaped vessels and a regular, narrow, evenly-spaced pigment network indicate a benign dermal/junctional nevus, and milia-like cysts indicate seborrheic keratosis.

B113.56.stem: On dermoscopy, which structure-to-histology correlation is correct?
