options.A: Streaks correspond to confluent junctional nests of melanocytes at the lesion margin
options.B: Blue-white veil corresponds to dilated, blood-filled vascular spaces in the upper dermis
options.C: Milia-like cysts correspond to pigmented basaloid-cell aggregates in the papillary dermis
options.D: Regression structures correspond to elongated, regular, evenly pigmented rete ridges

B113.56.exp: Each dermoscopic structure maps to a specific histologic substrate, and the correct pairing here is that streaks (radial lines/pseudopods at the lesion edge) correspond to confluent *junctional nests* of melanocytes at the dermo-epidermal junction; when these are irregularly distributed around the periphery they signal radial-growth-phase melanoma. Knowing the histologic correlate is what lets you reason about, rather than memorize, dermoscopy. The distractors give mismatched correlates: the blue-white veil reflects deep pigmented dermal melanocytes with epidermal hypergranulosis (not dilated vessels); milia-like cysts are intraepidermal keratin-filled horn pseudocysts seen in seborrheic keratosis (not basaloid cell aggregates); and regression structures (scar-like white areas, peppering) reflect dermal fibrosis and melanophages (not regular rete ridges).

B113.57.stem: On dermoscopy of a non-melanocytic lesion, arborizing (branched) vessels are the hallmark of which tumor?

options.A: Basal cell carcinoma
options.B: A seborrheic keratosis
options.C: A cherry hemangioma
options.D: Amelanotic melanoma

B113.57.exp: Arborizing vessels — sharply focused, bright-red, large-caliber telangiectasias that branch like a tree into progressively finer twigs — are the dermoscopic hallmark of basal cell carcinoma; they lie in the superficial dermis under a translucent tumor, which is why they appear so crisp. Recognizing them lets you separate a pigmented BCC from melanoma even when both look brown clinically, since each non-melanocytic tumor carries its own vascular/structural signature. The distractors are those competing signatures: seborrheic keratosis shows milia-like cysts and comedo-like openings (with hairpin vessels); a hemangioma/vascular lesion shows red-blue lacunae (sharply bordered red saccules); and melanoma shows atypical, dotted or irregular polymorphous vessels together with the blue-white veil.
B113.57.hy: Arborizing vessels = BCC; each non-melanocytic tumor has its own vascular pattern.

B113.58.stem: Two non-invasive imaging tools are FDA-approved for melanoma. Which pairing of test and performance is correct?

options.A: RCM ~92% sensitivity/~70% specificity; electrical impedance spectroscopy >96% sensitivity/~35% specificity
options.B: Both reflectance confocal microscopy and impedance spectroscopy exceed 95% specificity and replace histopathology
options.C: Electrical impedance spectroscopy has the higher specificity of the two and rarely yields false-positive results
options.D: Reflectance confocal microscopy images only structures lying below the reticular dermis and subcutis

B113.58.exp: Two non-invasive adjuncts are FDA-approved for melanoma diagnosis, and they trade off sensitivity against specificity. Reflectance confocal microscopy (RCM) uses a near-infrared laser to image cells in the epidermis and papillary dermis at near-histologic resolution *in vivo* ("optical biopsy"), giving a balanced pooled sensitivity ~92% and specificity ~70%. Electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) instead measures how skin resists weak alternating currents — malignant cells alter tissue impedance — yielding very high sensitivity (>96%) but poor specificity (~35%), because benign lesions such as seborrheic keratoses also distort impedance and generate frequent false positives. Both are triage tools that reduce unnecessary biopsies but neither replaces histopathology. The distractors are false: neither supplants histology, RCM (not EIS) is the more *specific* test, and RCM images only the superficial skin, not below the reticular dermis.

B113.59.stem: Which histopathologic feature favors melanoma over a benign melanocytic nevus?

options.A: Absence of maturation — dermal nests and nuclei fail to become smaller with descent
options.B: Architectural symmetry with sharp, well-defined lateral circumscription of the lesion
options.C: Progressive maturation, with smaller nests and nuclei in the deeper dermal component
options.D: Evenly spaced, uniformly sized and shaped junctional nests along the rete ridges

B113.59.exp: "Maturation" is the normal phenomenon in a benign nevus whereby melanocytes get smaller, lose pigment, and form smaller nests as they descend deeper into the dermis (so the lesion looks "calmer" at its base). The defining histologic clue to melanoma is the *absence* of this maturation — dermal nests and nuclei remain large and atypical at depth — together with overall asymmetry, poor lateral circumscription, and pagetoid (upward, single-cell) scatter of melanocytes through the upper epidermis. Other supporting features are deep/atypical mitoses and confluent, irregularly sized and spaced nests. The distractors instead list the architecture of a *benign* nevus: symmetry, sharp circumscription, progressive maturation with descent, and evenly spaced uniform nests.
B113.59.hy: Melanoma = no maturation with descent (+ asymmetry, pagetoid scatter).

B113.60.stem: In the two-phase model of melanoma growth, which statement is correct?

options.A: The vertical growth phase (dermal nests larger than intraepidermal cells) confers metastatic potential
options.B: The radial (horizontal) growth phase is the phase that confers metastatic potential, even when in situ
options.C: Pagetoid scatter of single melanocytes through the epidermis is what defines the vertical growth phase
options.D: Growth phase (radial vs vertical) is a required, scored component of current AJCC-8 melanoma staging

B113.60.exp: In the two-phase (Clark) model, the radial growth phase (RGP) is intraepidermal and/or superficially papillary-dermal spread with no expansile dermal nodule — single cells and small nests that lack the capacity to metastasize, even when a few cells focally enter the papillary dermis. The vertical growth phase (VGP) is the biologic turning point: an expansile dermal nest or nodule whose cells are larger than, or cytologically/architecturally distinct from, the intraepidermal melanocytes (often with dermal mitoses), and it is this clone that acquires metastatic competence — which is why VGP melanomas can seed lymphatics and blood. Conceptually, RGP is "horizontal/non-metastasizing" and VGP is "deep/metastasizing." The distractors invert this (the RGP is not the metastatic phase), misdefine pagetoid scatter (which is an *intraepidermal*, in-situ feature, not the vertical phase), or wrongly state that growth phase is a formal AJCC staging parameter (it is not — staging uses Breslow thickness, ulceration, and mitotic context, not RGP/VGP).
B113.60.hy: VGP = metastatic potential; RGP generally lacks it; growth phase is not in AJCC staging.

B113.61.stem: Which immunohistochemical marker is most sensitive for melanocytes (including spindled/desmoplastic melanoma) and is favored for its easily interpreted nuclear staining?

options.A: SOX10
options.B: HMB45 (gp100)
options.C: MART-1/Melan-A
options.D: CD68

B113.61.exp: SOX10 is a neural-crest nuclear transcription factor expressed by melanocytes (and Schwann cells), so it is highly sensitive for melanocytic lineage — crucially including the poorly differentiated spindled cells of desmoplastic melanoma, where the melanosome-based markers HMB45/MART-1/MITF are characteristically negative. It is favored over S100 (a calcium-binding protein that is also sensitive but stains many other cell types — Langerhans cells, Schwann cells, fat, eccrine glands) because its crisp *nuclear* pattern is easier to read and produces far less background staining of dermal fibrohistiocytic cells, which is exactly what helps distinguish residual desmoplastic melanoma from scar. Neither S100 nor SOX10 is perfectly melanocyte-specific, so they confirm lineage rather than malignancy. The distractors are less suitable: HMB45 (recognizes gp100/PMEL17) and MART-1/Melan-A are less sensitive and often negative in desmoplastic melanoma, and CD68 is a histiocyte/macrophage marker that does not label melanocytes at all.
B113.61.hy: SOX10 = sensitive nuclear melanocyte marker (catches desmoplastic), easier than S100.

B113.62.stem: PRAME immunohistochemistry is useful in melanocytic lesions because:

options.A: It is usually negative in benign nevi but diffusely nuclear-positive in most melanomas (desmoplastic/spitzoid excepted)
options.B: It is positive in essentially all benign nevi while remaining negative in the majority of true melanomas
options.C: It reliably stains desmoplastic melanoma in nearly every case, making it the marker of choice for that subtype
options.D: It is a cytoplasmic marker that is specific for keratinocytes rather than for cells of melanocytic lineage

B113.62.exp: PRAME (PReferentially expressed Antigen in MElanoma) is a cancer-testis antigen first identified in melanoma-reactive T cells; on immunohistochemistry it is generally *negative* in benign nevi, solar lentigines, and melanocytic hyperplasia of sun-damaged skin, but shows *diffuse nuclear positivity* in the majority of primary cutaneous melanomas and their metastases — and this pattern correlates well with abnormal cytogenetics. That benign-negative/malignant-positive split is what makes it useful for assessing melanoma surgical margins (including lentigo maligna), distinguishing melanoma micrometastases from benign nodal nevi in sentinel nodes, and adjudicating ambiguous melanocytic lesions. Important caveats temper it: only about a third of desmoplastic melanomas are PRAME-positive, spitzoid neoplasms correlate less reliably, and a minority of benign nevi can be positive, so PRAME must always be read alongside conventional H&E. The distractors invert the staining pattern (positive in nevi, negative in melanoma), overstate desmoplastic positivity, or wrongly call PRAME a keratinocyte marker.
B113.62.hy: PRAME: negative in nevi, positive in most melanoma; weak in desmoplastic/spitzoid.

B113.63.stem: Which statement about molecular tests used to classify ambiguous melanocytic lesions is correct?

options.A: CGH screens the whole genome for copy-number change, whereas FISH targets a few loci (6p25/RREB1, 11q13/CCND1, 9p21)
options.B: FISH provides broad genome-wide, hypothesis-free copy-number screening across all chromosomes simultaneously
options.C: CGH reliably detects single-nucleotide point mutations as well as balanced reciprocal translocations and fusions
options.D: Gene expression profiling works by sequencing the entire genome of the tumor to identify its driver mutations

B113.63.exp: The two cytogenetic tools differ chiefly in breadth. Comparative genomic hybridization (CGH) co-hybridizes tumor and reference DNA to detect DNA copy-number gains and losses across the *whole genome* in a single hypothesis-free assay — benign nevi typically show none, whereas most melanomas show characteristic gains (e.g. 6p, 7, 8q) and losses (e.g. 6q, 9p, 10q). Fluorescence in-situ hybridization (FISH) is *targeted*: labeled probes interrogate only a few preselected loci known to be aberrant in melanoma — classically 6p25 (RREB1), 6q23 (MYB), 11q13 (CCND1), and 9p21 (CDKN2A) — so it is faster and works on small samples but can miss aberrations outside those probes. Both detect copy-number change, so neither reliably finds point mutations (e.g. BRAF V600E) or balanced translocations. The distractors invert the roles (FISH is the targeted one, not genome-wide), overstate CGH's reach (it misses point mutations and balanced rearrangements), or misdefine gene-expression profiling (GEP measures mRNA expression of a selected gene set, not whole-genome sequencing).
B113.63.hy: CGH = genome-wide copy number; FISH = a few targeted loci.

B113.64.stem: In distinguishing Spitz neoplasms from melanoma, which molecular finding actually favors a Spitz lesion rather than conventional melanoma?

options.A: An isolated gain of chromosome 11p (HRAS) or an isolated loss of chromosome 3 (BAP1)
options.B: A homozygous deletion of the 9p21 (CDKN2A) tumor-suppressor locus by FISH testing
options.C: A TERT promoter mutation, which is associated with extranodal metastatic spread
options.D: A BRAF V600E mutation accompanied by multiple chromosomal copy-number changes

B113.64.exp: When separating a Spitz neoplasm from conventional melanoma, a few cytogenetic findings are reassuring because they characterize Spitz biology and are *not* seen in ordinary melanoma: about 20% of Spitz nevi show an isolated copy-number gain of 11p (which corresponds to HRAS activation) or an isolated loss of chromosome 3 (corresponding to BAP1 loss), and either, in isolation, favors a Spitz lesion rather than melanoma. The logic is that Spitz tumors are defined by specific driver events (HRAS, or kinase fusions such as ALK/ROS1/NTRK) and a *quiet* genome, whereas melanoma accumulates many aberrations. The distractors instead point toward malignancy: homozygous deletion of 9p21 (CDKN2A), a TERT-promoter mutation (telomerase reactivation), and BRAF V600E accompanied by multiple copy-number changes all argue for melanoma over a Spitz nevus.
B113.64.hy: Isolated 11p (HRAS) gain or chr-3/BAP1 loss favors Spitz, not melanoma.

B113.65.stem: In spitzoid neoplasms, which molecular finding is most associated with aggressive behavior (advanced locoregional disease or death)?

options.A: Homozygous deletion of 9p21 (CDKN2A)
options.B: Gain of chromosome 11p (HRAS)
options.C: Isolated loss of chromosome 3
options.D: A kinase fusion without copy-number change

B113.65.exp: Among the cytogenetic findings in spitzoid melanocytic neoplasms, homozygous (biallelic) deletion of 9p21 — the locus of the CDKN2A tumor suppressor, which encodes the cell-cycle brakes p16 and p14ARF — is the single change most strongly tied to aggressive behavior, i.e. advanced locoregional disease or death; complete loss of both copies removes a key checkpoint and de-restrains proliferation. For this reason, adding the 9p21/CEP9 probe to a melanoma FISH panel significantly increases sensitivity for spitzoid melanoma. A useful gradient: gains of 8q24 (cMYC) and 11q13 (CCND1) also predict an aggressive course but carry *lower* risk than homozygous 9p21 loss, and a TERT-promoter mutation likewise flags worse behavior. The distractors are either lower-risk or Spitz-favoring: isolated 11p (HRAS) gain and isolated chromosome-3 loss favor a benign Spitz nevus, and the 8q24/11q13 gains are real but second-tier compared with 9p21 deletion.

B113.66.stem: Subungual melanoma may present with longitudinal melanonychia. Pigment extending onto the proximal/lateral nail fold or hyponychium is termed:

options.A: The Hutchinson sign
options.B: The ugly-duckling sign
options.C: The Little Red Riding Hood sign
options.D: The EFG sign

B113.66.exp: Longitudinal melanonychia is a brown-black pigmented band running along the nail from the matrix to the free edge; while most bands are benign (ethnic, drug-induced, or a nail-matrix nevus), the Hutchinson sign — extension of that pigment beyond the nail plate onto the surrounding skin, i.e. the proximal/lateral nail fold or hyponychium — is the worrying clue that the underlying process is a subungual (acral lentiginous) melanoma invading periungual tissue. Its presence should prompt nail-matrix biopsy. The pathophysiologic point is that pigment "spilling" past the matrix onto skin reflects melanocytic proliferation beyond the nail unit, not a contained band. The distractors are unrelated named signs: the ugly-duckling sign (an outlier nevus), the "Little Red Riding Hood" sign (surrounding erythema), and the EFG rule (Elevated/Firm/Growing, for amelanotic nodular melanoma).
B113.66.hy: Hutchinson sign = periungual pigment spread in subungual melanoma.

B113.67.stem: A fair-skinned 50-year-old has this flat-to-slightly-raised lesion on the back showing asymmetry, irregular notched borders, and brown-black color variation. Which melanoma subtype does the morphology best represent?

options.A: Superficial spreading melanoma
options.B: Nodular melanoma
options.C: Lentigo maligna melanoma
options.D: Acral lentiginous melanoma

B113.67.exp: The recognition cue is a flat-to-slightly-raised, asymmetric plaque on the trunk with irregular notched borders and brown-black color variegation — the textbook look of superficial spreading melanoma (SSM, the low-CSD subtype and commonest melanoma in light skin). The flatness reflects its long radial (intraepidermal/superficial) growth phase, and the color variation reflects uneven melanin and partial regression. The same-class distractors are the other growth-pattern subtypes, separable here by morphology and site: nodular melanoma is a raised dome (vertical growth, not a flat plaque), lentigo maligna melanoma is a tan macule on the chronically sun-damaged face of the elderly, and acral lentiginous melanoma is on the palm, sole, or nail.
B113.67.hy: Flat asymmetric trunk lesion with color variation = SSM.
B113.67.src: Bologna Ch 113 · Fig 113.8, Superficial Spreading Melanoma

B113.68.stem: This darkly pigmented dome-shaped papule developed rapidly over weeks. Which subtype is most consistent with the appearance?

options.A: Nodular melanoma
options.B: Superficial spreading melanoma
options.C: Lentigo maligna melanoma
options.D: Dysplastic nevus

B113.68.exp: The recognition cue is a darkly pigmented, dome-shaped (often symmetric) papule/nodule that grew rapidly over weeks — the hallmark of nodular melanoma, which enters the vertical growth phase from the outset with no radial-phase lead-time, hence the fast, three-dimensional growth and a tendency to ulcerate or bleed. Because it skips the flat radial stage, it is often thick and dangerous when found, which is why "rapidly growing nodule" should trigger biopsy regardless of ABCDE. The distractors are other entities: SSM is a *flat* plaque with a radial phase, lentigo maligna is a facial macule on sun-damaged skin, and a dysplastic nevus is a flatter, more symmetric, stable benign lesion that does not balloon over weeks.
B113.68.hy: Rapidly growing pigmented nodule = nodular melanoma.

B113.69.stem: This asymmetric pigmented macule/plaque is on the face of an elderly man with chronic sun damage. Which subtype does it represent?

options.A: Lentigo maligna melanoma
options.B: Superficial spreading melanoma
options.C: Acral lentiginous melanoma
options.D: Nodular melanoma

B113.69.exp: The recognition cue is an asymmetric, variably pigmented macule/plaque on the chronically sun-damaged face of an elderly man — the classic presentation of lentigo maligna melanoma (LMM, the high-CSD subtype). The flat, ill-defined tan-brown patch on a background of solar elastosis reflects a slow lentiginous radial phase, and clinical margins typically extend beyond what the eye sees, complicating excision. The distractors differ by site and biology: SSM favors the trunk/legs of younger adults (intermittent sun), ALM occurs on acral/nail sites, and nodular melanoma is a raised, rapidly growing nodule rather than a facial macule.
B113.69.hy: Pigmented facial macule in elderly CSD skin = LMM.
B113.69.src: Bologna Ch 113 · Fig 113.13, Lentigo Maligna Melanoma

B113.70.stem: This irregularly pigmented lesion is on the plantar surface. Which subtype is most likely?

options.A: Acral lentiginous melanoma
options.B: Superficial spreading melanoma
options.C: Lentigo maligna melanoma
options.D: Blue nevus-like melanoma

B113.70.exp: The recognition cue is an irregularly pigmented macule/plaque on the plantar (sole) surface — the classic location for acral lentiginous melanoma (ALM), which arises on glabrous palmo-plantar/subungual skin independent of UV exposure and is the subtype that predominates *proportionally* in darker-skinned patients. On dermoscopy a parallel-ridge pattern (pigment along the raised ridges) supports the diagnosis, distinguishing it from the parallel-furrow pattern of a benign acral nevus. The same-class distractors are the other subtypes by site: SSM favors the trunk/legs, LMM the sun-damaged face, and blue nevus-like melanoma the scalp.
B113.70.hy: Pigmented plantar lesion = acral lentiginous melanoma.
B113.70.src: Bologna Ch 113 · Fig 113.15, Acral Lentiginous Melanoma

B113.71.stem: This nail shows a pigmented band with extension of pigment onto the proximal nail fold. The periungual extension is which clinical sign?

options.A: Hutchinson sign
options.B: Ugly-duckling sign
options.C: Little Red Riding Hood sign
options.D: EFG sign

B113.71.exp: The recognition cue is a pigmented nail band whose pigment spills beyond the nail plate onto the proximal nail fold/periungual skin — the Hutchinson sign, which flags a subungual (acral lentiginous) melanoma rather than a benign longitudinal melanonychia. The spread of pigment onto skin indicates melanocytic proliferation extending past the nail matrix and should prompt matrix biopsy. The distractors are unrelated named signs: ugly-duckling (an outlier nevus), "Little Red Riding Hood" (surrounding erythema), and EFG (Elevated/Firm/Growing, for amelanotic nodular melanoma).
B113.71.hy: Periungual pigment spread = Hutchinson sign (subungual melanoma).

B113.72.stem: This skin-colored to light-pink nodule lacked obvious pigment; biopsy confirmed melanoma 4 mm thick. Which descriptor fits?

options.A: Amelanotic melanoma
options.B: Lentigo maligna melanoma
options.C: Superficial spreading melanoma in situ
options.D: Dermal nevus

B113.72.exp: The recognition cue is a skin-colored to light-pink nodule with little or no visible pigment that nonetheless proves to be a thick (here 4 mm) melanoma — an amelanotic melanoma, which lacks the pigment-based ABCDE clues and is therefore easily dismissed as a benign nodule, basal cell carcinoma, or pyogenic granuloma. This pigment-poor pitfall is exactly why the EFG rule (Elevated, Firm, Growing) exists: biopsy a firm, enlarging pink nodule even without pigment. The distractors are pigmented or benign and so do not fit a non-pigmented thick nodule: LMM and SSM-in-situ are pigmented *macular* lesions, and an intradermal nevus is a benign, stable papule.

B113.73.stem: This blue-black, deeply situated nodule arose on the scalp; histology showed an associated cellular blue nevus. Which subtype is most likely?

options.A: Blue nevus-like melanoma
options.B: Nodular melanoma arising de novo
options.C: Acral lentiginous melanoma
options.D: Lentigo maligna melanoma

B113.73.exp: The recognition cue is a blue-black, deeply situated nodule on the scalp with histology showing an associated cellular blue nevus — blue nevus-like melanoma ("malignant blue nevus"). The blue-black color is the Tyndall effect of melanin sited deep in the dermis, and the blue-nevus component carries GNAQ/GNA11 mutations (with BAP1/SF3B1/EIF1AX changes marking malignant transformation); the entity is rare but aggressive. The same-class distractors differ by origin/site: a de novo nodular melanoma lacks the blue-nevus association, and ALM/LMM occur at acral and facial sites rather than as a deep scalp nodule.
B113.73.hy: Blue-black scalp nodule + cellular blue nevus = blue nevus-like melanoma.
B113.73.src: Bologna Ch 113 · Fig 113.19, Melanoma Arising in a Blue Nevus

B113.74.stem: This dermoscopic image of a melanoma shows a confluent gray-blue to whitish-blue structureless area. What is this feature called?

options.A: Blue-white veil
options.B: Milia-like cysts
options.C: Arborizing vessels
options.D: Red-blue lacunae

B113.74.exp: The recognition cue is a confluent gray-blue to whitish-blue *structureless* zone on dermoscopy — the blue-white veil, one of the most specific dermoscopic signs of melanoma. Its histologic correlate is heavily pigmented melanocytes/melanin deep in the dermis (blue, via the Tyndall effect) beneath an acanthotic epidermis with hypergranulosis (white), so it usually marks an invasive lesion. The same-class distractors are other dermoscopic structures with different "homes": milia-like cysts (seborrheic keratosis), arborizing vessels (basal cell carcinoma), and red-blue lacunae (vascular lesion/hemangioma).
B113.74.hy: Confluent blue-white structureless area = blue-white veil.

B113.75.stem: This H&E section shows atypical melanocytes as solitary units and irregular nests scattered at all levels of the epidermis. This pattern is termed:

options.A: Pagetoid scatter
options.B: Maturation with descent
options.C: Kamino bodies
options.D: Cowdry inclusions

B113.75.exp: The recognition cue is atypical melanocytes scattered as solitary units and irregular nests at *all* levels of the epidermis (not just the basal layer) — pagetoid scatter, a hallmark of melanoma; when the atypia is confined to the epidermis it defines melanoma in situ. The name comes from the upward "buckshot" spread resembling Paget cells; normal/benign melanocytes stay along the dermo-epidermal junction. The distractors are different concepts: maturation with descent is a *benign-nevus* feature (cells shrink as they go deeper), Kamino bodies (dull-pink dermo-epidermal globules) are a Spitz-nevus clue, and Cowdry inclusions are viral nuclear inclusions.

B113.76.stem: This H&E section from sun-damaged facial skin shows a lentiginous proliferation of single atypical melanocytes along the basal layer with extension down hair follicles and marked dermal solar elastosis. Which subtype's histology is shown?

options.A: Lentigo maligna
options.B: Superficial spreading melanoma
options.C: Nodular melanoma
options.D: Acral lentiginous melanoma

B113.76.exp: The recognition cue is a *lentiginous* proliferation of single atypical melanocytes strung along the basal layer (rather than broad pagetoid nests), with extension down hair-follicle/adnexal epithelium, on a background of marked dermal solar elastosis — the histology of lentigo maligna (high-CSD melanoma in situ on the face). The solar elastosis (degenerated, basophilic dermal collagen from chronic UV) and adnexal extension explain both the diagnosis and why margins are hard to clear. The same-class distractors are other subtypes' histology: SSM shows broad *pagetoid* (upward) spread without prominent elastosis, nodular melanoma is a dermal nodule with minimal epidermal component, and ALM shows lentiginous spread in a thickened acral epidermis.
B113.76.hy: Lentiginous single-cell spread + adnexal extension + solar elastosis = lentigo maligna.

B113.77.stem: This dermal tumor shows loosely textured spindle cells within a fibroblastic stroma with scattered lymphoid aggregates; HMB45 and MART-1 were negative but S100 was positive. Which diagnosis fits?

options.A: Desmoplastic melanoma
options.B: Nodular melanoma
options.C: Superficial spreading melanoma
options.D: Blue nevus

B113.77.exp: The recognition cue is a dermal tumor of loosely arranged spindle cells set in a fibroblastic (collagenous) stroma with scattered lymphoid aggregates, that is HMB45/MART-1 *negative* but S100 *positive* — the diagnostic signature of desmoplastic melanoma. The IHC profile is the giveaway and a classic pitfall: the spindled cells are too poorly differentiated to express melanosomal markers, so only the lineage marker S100 (or SOX10) lights up, and a scar or fibrohistiocytic lesion could otherwise be mistaken for it. The same-class distractors do *not* fit a spindle-cell, S100-only profile: nodular and superficial spreading melanoma are epithelioid and usually HMB45/MART-1-positive, and a blue nevus is a benign dendritic, heavily pigmented dermal lesion.
B113.77.hy: Spindle cells + fibrous stroma + S100+/HMB45−/MART-1− = desmoplastic melanoma.

B113.78.stem: This H&E section shows a relatively well-circumscribed dermal nodule of confluent atypical melanocytes with minimal overlying epidermal involvement. Which subtype's histology is shown?

options.A: Nodular melanoma
options.B: Lentigo maligna
options.C: Melanoma in situ
options.D: Acral lentiginous melanoma

B113.78.exp: The recognition cue is a relatively well-circumscribed *dermal* nodule of confluent atypical melanocytes with minimal overlying epidermal (in-situ) involvement — the histology of nodular melanoma, which by definition is essentially pure vertical growth with little or no adjacent radial (intraepidermal) component. The scant epidermal involvement is the key contrast: other subtypes leave a broad intraepidermal "shoulder," whereas NM dives straight into the dermis. The same-class distractors differ histologically: lentigo maligna and melanoma in situ are *intraepidermal* processes (no dermal nodule), and ALM shows lentiginous junctional spread under a thickened acral epidermis.
B113.78.hy: Dermal nodule, scant epidermal component = nodular melanoma histology.

B113.79.stem: On the dorsal foot of an 84-year-old man, this asymmetric, poorly circumscribed light-brown macule was the "outlier" lesion compared with the patient's other nevi. The lesion is best classified as:

options.A: Melanoma in situ
options.B: Invasive nodular melanoma
options.C: Solar lentigo
options.D: Dermatofibroma

B113.79.exp: The recognition cue is a subtle, asymmetric, poorly circumscribed light-brown macule on sun-damaged skin that stands out as the morphologic "outlier" among the patient's other nevi (the ugly-duckling principle) — a presentation best classified as melanoma in situ, i.e. early disease still confined to the epidermis before invasion. Such small, flat, featureless early melanomas are easily overlooked on naked-eye exam, which is exactly where comparative recognition and dermoscopy add value. The distractors differ morphologically: an invasive nodular melanoma would be a raised, palpable nodule, while a solar lentigo or dermatofibroma would be a benign, well-defined, stable lesion rather than an asymmetric outlier macule.
B113.79.hy: Subtle outlier macule on sun-damaged skin = melanoma in situ.

B113.80.stem: Which single histologic feature of a primary cutaneous melanoma is the strongest predictor of survival?

options.A: Breslow tumor thickness
options.B: Clark level of invasion
options.C: Dermal mitotic rate
options.D: Presence of tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes

B113.80.exp: Breslow thickness — the vertical extent of the tumor measured in millimeters — is the most important single prognostic factor in primary cutaneous melanoma and is the variable that defines the AJCC T-category, because the deeper the tumor invades the dermis, the greater its access to lymphatics and vasculature and the higher the risk of metastasis. This is why the entire T system is anchored on thickness breakpoints (1/2/4 mm) and why getting an accurate Breslow measurement from a complete excisional biopsy drives every downstream decision (margins, SLNB, surveillance intensity). The distractors are real prognostic concepts but weaker or superseded: Clark level grades the *anatomic* level of dermal invasion (papillary → reticular dermis → subcutis) and was largely abandoned because it is less reproducible and less predictive than thickness; dermal mitotic rate is independently prognostic (a high count lowers survival) but in AJCC-8 it no longer upstages the primary; and tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes are a secondary histologic feature reflecting host immune response, not the dominant survival determinant.
B113.80.hy: Breslow depth = strongest histologic survival predictor.

B113.81.stem: This schematic illustrates how melanoma thickness is measured. The double-headed arrow (the Breslow measurement) is taken from which landmark to which?

options.A: From the top of the granular layer (or ulcer base) to the deepest tumor cell
options.B: From the outer stratum corneum surface down to the dermal–subcutaneous junction
options.C: From the dermal–epidermal junction down to the deepest invasive tumor cell
options.D: By the anatomic level of invasion reached (papillary versus reticular dermis)

B113.81.exp: The Breslow method measures vertical tumor thickness with an ocular micrometer from the top of the granular cell layer of the epidermis down to the deepest point of tumor invasion; if the lesion is ulcerated (no intact epidermis to start from), measurement begins instead from the base of the ulcer. The granular layer is chosen as the upper landmark because it is a fixed, reproducible epidermal reference, and the deepest tumor cell is the lower landmark because depth correlates with metastatic potential — together they yield the single number that defines the T-category. The figure shows exactly these two reference points spanning the tumor. The distractors misplace the landmarks: full-thickness skin or measuring to the dermal–epidermal junction would not capture tumor depth, and describing invasion by *anatomic levels* of the dermis (papillary, reticular, subcutaneous fat) is the separate Clark level system, which is less reproducible and has been largely supplanted by Breslow thickness.
B113.81.hy: Breslow = granular layer (or ulcer base) → deepest tumor cell.

B113.82.stem: In AJCC-8, which Breslow thickness boundaries separate the T categories?
B113.82.exp: AJCC-8 sorts every invasive melanoma into four T-categories by Breslow thickness: T1 ≤1.0 mm, T2 >1.0–2.0 mm, T3 >2.0–4.0 mm, and T4 >4.0 mm — the 1/2/4 mm breakpoints are the backbone of the entire staging system because thickness is the dominant prognostic factor. These cut-points are not arbitrary trivia: each band carries a stepwise drop in survival and, combined with ulceration, drives whether SLNB is offered and how aggressively the patient is surveilled. Knowing them lets you reconstruct the "a/b" subcategories and the stage groupings around nodal status. The distractors use adjacent but wrong cut-points (0.8/1.6/3.0; 2/4/6; 1/3/5) — the classic numeric-staging trap, where a plausible-looking sequence is wrong by a fraction of a millimeter, so only the exact recalled figures survive.

B113.83.stem: Within T1, what distinguishes T1a from T1b in AJCC-8?

options.A: T1a = <0.8 mm no ulceration; T1b = <0.8 mm with ulceration OR 0.8–1.0 mm
options.B: T1a = <1.0 mm with any dermal mitoses; T1b = <1.0 mm without any mitoses
options.C: T1a = Clark anatomic level II invasion; T1b = Clark anatomic level III invasion
options.D: T1a = <0.5 mm thickness; T1b = 0.5–1.0 mm thickness, regardless of ulceration

B113.83.exp: AJCC-8 subdivides T1 using a 0.8 mm thickness threshold and the presence of ulceration: T1a is a thin melanoma <0.8 mm WITHOUT ulceration, whereas T1b is either <0.8 mm WITH ulceration OR any tumor 0.8–1.0 mm (regardless of ulceration). This split matters clinically because it is the line at which sentinel-node sampling becomes worth discussing — T1a melanomas have a <5% node-positivity rate so SLNB is not routine, while T1b crosses into the ~5–10% range where SLNB is considered. A major AJCC-8 change was *dropping* mitotic rate as a T1 splitter (it had defined T1b in AJCC-7), because the 0.8 mm cut-point proved more reproducible. The distractors invoke criteria AJCC-8 no longer uses for this purpose — mitotic rate, the abandoned Clark level, or a 0.5 mm cut-point that does not exist in the current T1 definition.
B113.83.hy: T1a <0.8 mm no ulcer; T1b <0.8 mm+ulcer OR 0.8–1.0 mm.

B113.84.stem: How does ulceration affect AJCC T-staging, and how is it defined histologically?

options.A: It upstages a tumor "a"→"b" within its thickness band; defined as loss of intact epidermis + host response
options.B: It lowers the assigned T category by one full level because it represents earlier, more superficial disease
options.C: It is determined purely clinically as a visibly eroded or crusted surface, without any histologic criterion
options.D: It has no effect on T-staging in AJCC-8 and is recorded only as an optional descriptive comment

B113.84.exp: Ulceration is a histologic finding — full-thickness absence of an intact epidermis over a major portion of the tumor, accompanied by a host reactive response (fibrin deposition, neutrophilic/inflammatory reaction) — and it is one of the most powerful adverse prognostic features in melanoma, on par with thickness. In the AJCC system it acts as the universal "upstaging" modifier: within every thickness band, the presence of ulceration moves a tumor from the "a" subcategory to the worse "b" subcategory (T1b, T2b, etc.), because ulcerated tumors metastasize and recur more often at any given depth. This is why pathology reports must explicitly state ulceration status, and why an ulcerated thin melanoma can warrant SLNB that a non-ulcerated one would not. The distractors are false: ulceration never lowers the T category, it is determined microscopically (not by clinical inspection), and it most definitely changes staging — that is its entire role.
B113.84.hy: Ulceration (histologic) = a→b upstaging within each T.

B113.85.stem: What is the current status of dermal mitotic rate in AJCC-8 melanoma staging?

options.A: It is prognostic but no longer upstages the primary (e.g. ≥11/mm² lowers 5-yr survival to ~84%)
options.B: It is a required staging component that directly defines the T-stage of every primary melanoma
options.C: A mitotic rate of ≥1/mm² now automatically reclassifies a T1a tumor as a higher-risk T1b tumor
options.D: It has been shown to carry no prognostic value at all and is therefore no longer reported by pathologists

B113.85.exp: Dermal mitotic rate (mitotic figures counted per mm² in the invasive component) was a formal staging criterion in AJCC-7, where ≥1/mm² upstaged a thin melanoma to T1b, but AJCC-8 *removed* it as a T-category determinant because the 0.8 mm thickness cut-point proved more reproducible. Crucially, removal from staging did not erase its biology — a high mitotic rate still independently predicts worse outcome (for example ≥11/mm² is associated with ~84% 5-year survival in node-negative disease), so it is still reported and weighed in risk discussions. The teaching point is the distinction between a factor that *defines a stage* and one that *informs prognosis*: mitotic rate is now the latter, not the former. The distractors either overstate its current role (claiming it still defines T-stage or automatically converts T1a to T1b) or wrongly strip it of any prognostic meaning.
B113.85.hy: Mitotic rate: prognostic but no longer upstages in AJCC-8.

B113.86.stem: Which factor is the principal determinant of the N category?

options.A: Number of tumor-involved nodes (1; 2–3; ≥4) plus occult-vs-detected status and satellite/in-transit disease
options.B: The Breslow thickness of the primary tumor measured from the granular layer to the deepest cell
options.C: The serum lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) level measured at the time of the initial staging work-up
options.D: The specific anatomic site of any distant metastases, such as skin, lung, liver, or central nervous system

B113.86.exp: The AJCC N category captures regional (lymphatic) spread and is built from three things: the *number* of tumor-involved regional nodes (1 = N1; 2–3 = N2; ≥4 or matted = N3), *how* that involvement was found (clinically occult, i.e. microscopic disease detected only on SLNB, versus clinically detected palpable/radiologic nodes — detected disease being worse), and the presence of non-nodal lymphatic deposits (satellite, in-transit, or microsatellite metastases, which carry the "c" suffix). These axes matter because nodal status — especially detected versus occult and node count — is the strongest determinant of stage III subgroup and of who needs adjuvant systemic therapy. The distractors belong to other categories entirely: Breslow thickness defines T, while serum LDH and the anatomic site of distant spread define M (stage IV).
B113.86.hy: N = node number (1/2–3/≥4) + occult vs detected + satellite/in-transit.

B113.87.stem: How are satellite and in-transit metastases defined and prognostically weighted in AJCC-8?

options.A: Satellites are ≤2 cm from the primary, in-transit lesions >2 cm but not past the nodes; both are lymphatic, similar prognosis
options.B: Satellites are >2 cm from the primary tumor whereas in-transit lesions lie within 2 cm of the excision scar
options.C: Both satellite and in-transit lesions are hematogenous visceral deposits and are classified as M1 distant disease
options.D: In-transit metastases carry a markedly better prognosis than satellite lesions despite both being lymphatic

B113.87.exp: Satellite and in-transit metastases are both forms of regional *lymphatic* spread of melanoma between the primary site and the draining nodal basin, distinguished only by distance: satellite (and microsatellite) deposits lie within 2 cm of the primary, whereas in-transit metastases lie more than 2 cm from the primary but still proximal to the regional nodes. AJCC-8 explicitly treats the 2 cm boundary as somewhat arbitrary, because biologically these are the same process — tumor traveling through dermal/subcutaneous lymphatics — and they confer a similarly poor prognosis, which is why both are grouped under the N category (N1c/N2c/N3c) rather than M. Recognizing them as stage III lymphatic (not distant) disease is what changes management toward restaging, possible SLNB, and adjuvant therapy. The distractors fail by reversing the two distances, miscalling these deposits hematogenous M1 (distant) disease, or wrongly assigning them divergent prognoses.
B113.87.hy: Satellite ≤2 cm, in-transit >2 cm; both lymphatic, similar prognosis.

B113.88.stem: A patient has in-transit metastases but no tumor-involved lymph nodes. Which N category applies?

options.A: N1c
options.B: N0
options.C: N2a
options.D: N3b

B113.88.exp: In AJCC-8 the "c" suffix on an N category specifically flags non-nodal lymphatic disease (satellite, microsatellite, or in-transit metastases), and N1c is the special case where such deposits are present but NO regional lymph node is tumor-involved. This category exists because a patient can have lymphatic spread without a positive node, yet that still represents stage III disease deserving the same restaging and adjuvant-therapy discussion as nodal stage III. Understanding the N-suffix logic (number of nodes gives N1/N2/N3; "a" occult, "b" detected, "c" non-nodal lymphatic) lets you decode any N code on a report. The distractors are different categories: N0 is no regional disease at all, N2a is 2–3 *clinically occult* involved nodes, and N3b denotes ≥4 nodes or matted nodes — none of which is the node-negative-but-satellite scenario N1c describes.
B113.88.hy: N1c = satellite/in-transit disease with no involved nodes.

B113.89.stem: In AJCC-8 M classification, which mapping of metastatic site to M category is correct?

options.A: M1a = skin/soft tissue/non-regional node; M1b = lung; M1c = non-CNS visceral; M1d = CNS
options.B: M1a = lung; M1b = liver; M1c = brain; M1d = bone marrow and the skeletal system
options.C: M1a = CNS; M1b = visceral organs; M1c = lung; M1d = skin and distant soft tissue
options.D: M1a = any visceral organ; M1b = skin only; M1c = node only; M1d = lung only

B113.89.exp: The AJCC-8 M-category (stage IV) is organized by the anatomic site of distant metastasis, in ascending order of poor prognosis: M1a = distant skin, soft tissue, or non-regional lymph node; M1b = lung; M1c = other non-CNS visceral organs (liver, etc.); and M1d = central nervous system (brain), historically the worst site. Each is further appended with an LDH suffix — (0) for normal and (1) for elevated serum LDH — because elevated LDH independently signals high tumor burden and worse survival within any site. This site-plus-LDH structure matters because it shapes prognosis and modality choice (e.g. brain disease drives MRI surveillance and SRS). The distractors simply scramble these site-to-letter assignments, which is the precise recall trap on this item.
B113.89.hy: M1a skin/soft tissue, M1b lung, M1c visceral, M1d CNS.

B113.90.stem: What is the role of serum lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) in melanoma staging?

options.A: It subclassifies stage IV (the M-category suffix) and predicts survival in advanced disease
options.B: It defines the thickness boundary that separates T2 tumors from thicker T3 tumors
options.C: It is recommended as a routine baseline screening test for all newly diagnosed melanomas
options.D: It is the laboratory value that determines whether sentinel lymph node biopsy is indicated

B113.90.exp: Serum LDH (lactate dehydrogenase), a marker of high cell turnover and tumor burden, has been a formal component of stage IV subclassification since the 2002 AJCC revision — it forms the (0)/(1) suffix on the M-category — because an elevated level independently predicts worse survival and poorer treatment response in metastatic disease. Its value is therefore confined to *advanced* melanoma, where it refines prognosis beyond metastatic site alone. The reason it is not used earlier is biological and statistical: in localized melanoma LDH has low sensitivity and specificity for occult metastasis, so screening with it generates false positives without changing care. The distractors misapply it — LDH does not define any T thickness boundary, is explicitly not recommended as a baseline lab in early disease, and plays no role in deciding who gets a sentinel-node biopsy.
B113.90.hy: LDH = stage IV M-suffix; advanced-disease survival marker.

B113.91.stem: Which description of the AJCC-8 stage groupings is correct?

options.A: Stage 0 = in situ; I–II = localized; III = regional nodal/lymphatic; IV = distant metastasis
options.B: Stage 0 = localized invasive disease; stage I = in situ; III = distant; IV = nodal metastasis
options.C: Stage I = regional nodal disease; II = in-transit metastasis; III = in situ; IV = localized
options.D: Stage II = distant metastasis; III = melanoma in situ; IV = localized invasive primary tumor

B113.91.exp: The four-tier melanoma stage framework maps directly onto disease extent: stage 0 is melanoma *in situ* (confined to the epidermis, essentially 100% curable by excision); stages I–II are localized invasive cutaneous disease (node-negative, distinguished from each other mainly by thickness and ulceration); stage III is regional spread — tumor-involved lymph nodes and/or non-nodal lymphatic deposits (satellite/in-transit/microsatellite); and stage IV is distant metastasis. This progression is the conceptual scaffold for everything else: it determines whether the patient needs only excision (0–II), restaging plus adjuvant therapy (III), or systemic checkpoint/targeted therapy (IV). The distractors scramble these definitions, e.g. labeling regional disease as distant or localized disease as in situ, which collapses the very gradient the staging is meant to encode.
B113.91.hy: 0 in situ · I–II localized · III regional · IV distant.

B113.92.stem: Which set of AJCC-8 5-year melanoma-specific survival figures is correct?

options.A: Stage IA ~99%; thick ulcerated T4bN0 ~82%; stage IIID ~32%
options.B: Stage IA ~75%; thick ulcerated T4bN0 ~50%; stage IIID ~10%
options.C: Stage IA ~99%; thick ulcerated T4bN0 ~99%; stage IIID ~80%
options.D: Stage IA ~60%; thick ulcerated T4bN0 ~40%; stage IIID ~5%

B113.92.exp: The AJCC-8 melanoma-specific survival (MSS) data trace a steep prognostic gradient that is worth anchoring with a few landmark numbers: stage IA (thin, non-ulcerated, node-negative) has ~99% 5-year MSS, a thick ulcerated but still node-negative tumor (T4b N0, stage IIC) drops to ~82%, and the worst regional subgroup IIID falls to ~32% — even before reaching distant disease. These anchors teach the two levers that drive prognosis: primary-tumor factors (thickness and ulceration) separate the early stages, while nodal tumor burden separates the stage III subgroups, so a node-negative thick tumor can actually outperform a heavily node-positive thin one. Carrying a rough mental scale (near-100% early → 80s for high-risk localized → 30s for advanced regional) lets you sanity-check any survival figure on the exam. The distractors are uniformly wrong magnitudes — too pessimistic for early disease or implausibly optimistic for advanced regional disease.

B113.93.stem: Once nodal status is known, which factor is described as the strongest single predictor of survival and recurrence in primary cutaneous melanoma?

options.A: The pathologic status of the sentinel lymph node(s)
options.B: The patient's chronologic age at the time of diagnosis
options.C: The anatomic body site of the primary melanoma
options.D: The maximum clinical diameter of the primary lesion

B113.93.exp: Once a melanoma is invasive enough to warrant nodal evaluation (roughly ≥1 mm, or thinner with high-risk features), the pathologic status of the sentinel lymph node becomes the single strongest predictor of both survival and recurrence — a positive node identifies occult stage III disease and roughly stratifies patients into dramatically different prognostic groups. This is the entire rationale for offering SLNB: not to treat, but to stage, because knowing nodal status is more informative than any clinical variable and now also gates eligibility for adjuvant systemic therapy. It outranks the primary-tumor and patient factors precisely because it directly samples whether the tumor has already left the skin. The distractors — patient age, anatomic site, and clinical diameter — are real, independently adverse prognostic variables, but each is weaker than the biological information carried by the sentinel node itself.

B113.94.stem: In localized (stage I/II) melanoma, which clinical variables independently worsen prognosis?

options.A: Male sex, older age, and a trunk/head/neck (rather than extremity) primary site
options.B: Female sex together with a distal extremity location of the primary tumor
options.C: Younger patient age combined with a scalp location, but only in women
options.D: Left-sided versus right-sided location of the primary cutaneous tumor

B113.94.exp: Beyond the histologic drivers (thickness, ulceration), several *clinical* variables independently predict worse outcome in localized (node-negative) melanoma: male sex, older age, and an axial location — trunk or head/neck, with scalp melanomas notoriously aggressive — whereas female sex and extremity tumors fare comparatively better. These associations are partly biological (axial lesions may have richer or more ambiguous lymphatic drainage and present later) and partly behavioral (delayed detection on the back/scalp). They explain why the same-thickness tumor can carry different risk depending on who has it and where it sits, and why surveillance is sometimes intensified for older men with axial lesions. The distractors either invert these well-established associations (claiming extremity or female disease is worse) or invent prognostically irrelevant features such as laterality (left vs right).
B113.94.hy: Worse in localized melanoma: male sex, older age, trunk/head/neck site.

B113.95.stem: Among stage III patients, which nodal feature confers a worse prognosis?

options.A: Clinically detectable (palpable, macroscopic) nodal disease rather than occult microscopic disease
options.B: Microscopic disease detected only on sentinel-node biopsy rather than clinically palpable adenopathy
options.C: A single tumor-involved regional lymph node rather than four or more tumor-involved regional nodes
options.D: Clinically occult disease without satellites rather than the presence of multiple matted regional nodes

B113.95.exp: Within stage III, tumor burden in the nodes is a key prognostic axis, and one of its expressions is *how* the nodal disease was found: clinically detected (palpable or radiologically apparent, i.e. macroscopic) nodal metastasis carries a distinctly worse prognosis than clinically occult disease found only microscopically on sentinel-node biopsy. The reason is that a node large enough to be felt or imaged generally harbors more tumor and reflects a more advanced biology than a microscopic deposit, which is exactly why AJCC separates the "a" (occult) and "b" (detected) suffixes. This is also why finding occult disease early via SLNB, before it becomes palpable, is prognostically meaningful. The distractors either invert this relationship (claiming occult disease is worse) or substitute the *node-count* axis (fewer vs more nodes — true on its own, but not the occult-versus-detected distinction being tested).
B113.95.hy: Macroscopic (palpable) nodes worse than occult (microscopic) disease.

B113.96.stem: In stage IV melanoma, which is the major prognostic factor, and what was the historical outlook before modern therapy?

options.A: Site of distant mets (visceral worse); historically ~9-month median survival, <10% 5-yr with elevated LDH
options.B: Primary tumor diameter; historically a 5-year survival of roughly 50% even with visceral spread present
options.C: Patient biologic sex; historically a median survival of about 5 years regardless of metastatic burden
options.D: The number of separate primary melanomas; historically an overall cure rate of approximately 80%

B113.96.exp: In stage IV melanoma the dominant prognostic factor is the anatomic site of distant metastasis — non-visceral disease (distant skin, subcutaneous tissue, distant nodes; M1a) does best, while visceral spread (lung, then other organs, then CNS) progressively worsens outcome — and an elevated serum LDH further marks high burden and poor survival within any site. These are exactly the variables the M-subclassification encodes (M1a–M1d plus the LDH suffix), so the staging itself is built around prognosis. Historically, before modern systemic therapy, metastatic melanoma carried a median survival of only ~9 months and <10% 5-year survival when LDH was high — figures that have improved substantially in the checkpoint-inhibitor/targeted-therapy era, which is why these numbers are framed as the pre-modern baseline. The distractors cite the wrong drivers (e.g. primary thickness, which no longer dominates once distant disease is present) or wrong survival magnitudes.
B113.96.hy: Stage IV: visceral worse; historical median ~9 mo, <10% 5-yr if LDH high.

B113.97.stem: What do guidelines recommend regarding baseline imaging/laboratory tests in an asymptomatic patient with newly diagnosed cutaneous melanoma?

options.A: They recommend AGAINST routine baseline imaging or labs at any thickness in asymptomatic patients
options.B: They advise whole-body FDG PET-CT for every newly diagnosed patient regardless of tumor thickness
options.C: They advise a baseline contrast-enhanced brain MRI for all patients with T1 (≤1 mm) melanomas
options.D: They advise routine baseline serum LDH and a staging CT scan for all melanoma in-situ lesions

B113.97.exp: For an asymptomatic patient with newly diagnosed cutaneous melanoma — at any Breslow thickness — evidence-based guidelines recommend AGAINST routine baseline whole-body imaging and broad blood panels, because synchronous occult distant disease is rare at presentation and indiscriminate testing produces far more false positives (leading to needless biopsies, anxiety, and cost) than true findings. The exception is symptom-directed or stage-directed workup: imaging is pursued when history/exam suggests metastasis, or as *surveillance* once the patient is high-stage (IIB and above), not as a reflex at diagnosis. This "don't over-test early disease" principle is one of the most testable management pearls in the chapter. The distractors prescribe exactly the routine baseline CT/PET/LDH screening that the guidelines explicitly reject for asymptomatic patients.
B113.97.hy: No baseline imaging/labs in asymptomatic melanoma, any thickness.
B113.97.src: Bologna Ch 113 · Laboratory Investigations and Imaging

B113.98.stem: Per NCCN/AAD guidance, surveillance cross-sectional imaging (e.g. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis or PET-CT) in asymptomatic patients may be considered starting at which stage?

options.A: Stage IIB and higher
options.B: Stage 0 (in situ)
options.C: Stage IA
options.D: Only after distant metastasis is confirmed

B113.98.exp: While baseline imaging is discouraged in low-risk disease, NCCN/AAD guidance *does* consider periodic surveillance imaging — contrast CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis or whole-body FDG PET-CT — once a patient reaches stage IIB or higher, because the recurrence risk in that group is high enough that catching asymptomatic relapse early may change management. The stage IIB cutoff is the inflection point: below it the yield is too low to justify radiation/cost, while above it (including all stage III) the pretest probability of relapse rises substantially. This is the natural counterpart to the "no routine baseline imaging" rule — the difference is risk stratification by stage, not a blanket policy. The distractors err in the opposite directions: applying imaging to genuinely low-risk disease (in situ or stage IA) or, conversely, withholding it until metastasis is already proven, which defeats the surveillance purpose.
B113.98.hy: Surveillance imaging considered from stage IIB upward.
B113.98.src: Bologna Ch 113 · Laboratory Investigations and Imaging

B113.99.stem: Which imaging modality has the highest sensitivity for detecting distant metastatic melanoma at staging?

options.A: Whole-body FDG PET-CT (~80% sensitivity for distant disease)
options.B: Plain chest radiography of the thorax in two projections
options.C: A plain-film skeletal radiographic survey of the axial bones
options.D: Regional nodal-basin ultrasound (best for nodal, not distant, disease)

B113.99.exp: Whole-body FDG PET-CT (positron-emission tomography fused with CT, which highlights metabolically active tumor) is the most accurate single modality for detecting *distant* metastatic melanoma, with the highest reported sensitivity (~80%) and specificity (~87%), so it is preferred for high-stage staging and surveillance when available. Its whole-body coverage is the advantage — it can find unsuspected visceral or soft-tissue deposits in one study — but it is not the right tool for every question, which is the testable nuance. The distractors fail because each is suited to a different job: chest radiography is too insensitive for staging, a skeletal survey is not a melanoma staging tool, and nodal ultrasound — though excellent for following the regional *nodal* basin (and the backbone of post-SLNB nodal surveillance) — is not the modality for distant disease.
B113.99.hy: PET-CT = best for distant mets (~80% sens); nodal US best for nodes.
B113.99.src: Bologna Ch 113 · Laboratory Investigations and Imaging

B113.100.stem: Why is routine serum LDH not recommended in lower-stage melanoma?

options.A: It has low sensitivity and specificity for detecting metastasis in early-stage disease
options.B: It is far too costly a test to justify ordering in routine lower-stage follow-up
options.C: It becomes elevated only in melanoma in situ and not in invasive or metastatic disease
options.D: It reliably replaces sentinel-node biopsy for nodal staging and so is redundant here

B113.100.exp: Routine serum LDH is not recommended for lower-stage melanoma because, as a screening test for occult metastasis, it has poor sensitivity and specificity — it neither reliably detects early spread nor rules it out, so it does not change management in localized disease. Its one validated role is in stage IV, where the LDH level (normal vs elevated) forms part of the M-subclassification and refines prognosis; in some European centers the serum marker S100B is additionally used for surveillance of higher-risk patients. The general principle — reserve a test for the population in which it actually performs — is the teaching point. The distractors are false rationales: the issue is test performance, not cost; LDH is not a marker of in-situ disease; and it does not substitute for sentinel-node biopsy, which answers a completely different (regional-nodal) question.
B113.100.hy: LDH low sens/spec early → reserved for stage IV.

B113.101.stem: Which imaging modality is preferred for evaluating suspected brain metastases in melanoma?

options.A: MRI with contrast
options.B: Chest radiography
options.C: Nodal-basin ultrasound
options.D: Bone scan

B113.101.exp: Brain is a relatively common and dangerous site of melanoma metastasis (the M1d category), and contrast-enhanced MRI is the preferred imaging because its soft-tissue resolution detects small parenchymal and leptomeningeal deposits that CT can miss; contrast CT of the head is an acceptable alternative when MRI is unavailable or contraindicated. Accurate brain imaging matters because CNS disease changes both prognosis and treatment (e.g. stereotactic radiosurgery, choice/sequencing of systemic agents that cross the blood–brain barrier). Matching the modality to the target organ is the broader skill being tested. The distractors are mismatched to the brain: a chest radiograph images the thorax, nodal ultrasound follows the regional lymphatic basin, and a bone scan targets the skeleton — none assesses intracranial disease.

B113.102.stem: What is the preferred initial biopsy of a lesion suspected to be melanoma, and why?

options.A: Excisional/complete biopsy with narrow (1–3 mm) margins and adequate depth, allowing accurate microstaging
options.B: A superficial shave biopsy sampling only the single most darkly pigmented portion of the lesion
options.C: Fine-needle aspiration of the lesion to obtain cells for cytologic and immunocytochemical analysis
options.D: An incisional wedge biopsy of the thinnest portion of the lesion to minimize the resulting scar

B113.102.exp: The standard for biopsying a suspected melanoma is a complete (excisional) biopsy with narrow 1–3 mm margins taken down to a depth that includes the full tumor base, because melanoma is microstaged by Breslow thickness and the pathologist needs the *entire* lesion to measure depth, assess ulceration, and judge architecture without sampling error. Narrow margins are used (rather than a wide excision up front) so that the definitive wide local excision and any sentinel-node mapping can still be planned correctly afterward based on the true thickness. Getting this first step right is what protects every subsequent staging decision. The distractors all compromise microstaging: a partial superficial shave of one area, fine-needle aspiration (yields cells but no architecture or depth), or a thin incisional wedge risk transecting the tumor base and under-measuring Breslow thickness — the classic cause of under-staging.
B113.102.hy: Complete/excisional biopsy (1–3 mm margins) for accurate microstaging.
B113.102.src: Bologna Ch 113 · Skin Investigation and Clinical Diagnosis

B113.103.stem: Why are superficial shave biopsies generally avoided for a thick or palpable pigmented lesion?

options.A: They risk transecting the tumor base and so underestimating the Breslow thickness
options.B: They cannot provide any usable tissue for routine histopathologic examination at all
options.C: They almost always seed and disseminate melanoma cells along the biopsy track
options.D: They are contraindicated mainly because of the substantial bleeding risk they carry

B113.103.exp: A superficial shave biopsy removes only the upper portion of a lesion, so on a thick or palpable melanoma it risks slicing through the tumor before its deepest extent — transecting the base — which makes the measured Breslow thickness an underestimate and corrupts the staging that flows from it (margins, SLNB eligibility, prognosis). The chapter does permit a *broad* shave (saucerization) for thin, flat melanoma in situ of the lentigo maligna type, where there is little depth to lose, illustrating that the rule is depth-dependent, not an absolute ban. The unifying concept is preserving an accurate depth measurement. The distractors are false: shave specimens still yield histology, there is no evidence shave biopsy seeds melanoma along the wound, and the legitimate concern is tumor transection — not bleeding.
B113.103.hy: Avoid superficial shave on palpable lesions → risks transection/under-staging.

B113.104.stem: A patient has an invasive melanoma 2.5 mm thick (>2–4 mm). What is the recommended surgical (wide local excision) margin?

options.A: 2.0 cm
options.B: 0.5 cm
options.C: 1.0 cm
options.D: 4.0 cm

B113.104.exp: Wide local excision margins for melanoma scale with Breslow thickness, but they plateau: a melanoma >2 mm thick is excised with a 2.0 cm clinical margin, which is the maximum recommended for any thickness because randomized controlled trials comparing wider versus narrower margins showed no survival benefit beyond 2 cm even for thick tumors — wider excisions only add morbidity (larger defects, grafts) without reducing recurrence or death. This is why the historical 4–5 cm "radical" margins were abandoned. Remembering the ladder (in situ 0.5–1 cm; ≤1 mm → 1 cm; 1–2 mm → 1–2 cm; >2 mm → 2 cm) lets you assign the correct margin for any case. The distractors are the margins that belong to *thinner* tumors (0.5–1 cm for in situ/≤1 mm) or the obsolete 4 cm margin that trials proved unnecessary.

B113.105.stem: What is the recommended margin for a melanoma in situ (non-LM type), and what is the maximum WLE margin recommended for any thickness?

options.A: 0.5–1.0 cm for in situ; 2.0 cm maximum for thick melanoma (no benefit beyond)
options.B: 1.0 cm for melanoma in situ; up to a 5.0 cm maximum margin for thick tumors
options.C: 2.0 cm for melanoma in situ; up to a 4.0 cm maximum margin for thick tumors
options.D: 0.5 cm for melanoma in situ; a 1.0 cm maximum margin for the thickest tumors

B113.105.exp: Melanoma in situ — tumor confined to the epidermis with no invasion — is excised with 0.5–1.0 cm margins, and across all thicknesses 2.0 cm is the *maximum* useful wide-local-excision margin, because randomized trials demonstrated no added survival or recurrence benefit from going wider. The two facts together define the boundaries of the margin ladder: the floor (in situ, sub-centimeter) and the ceiling (2 cm). The point is that margin selection is evidence-capped, not surgeon-preference: bigger is not better once 2 cm is reached, only more morbid. The distractors inflate the ceiling — 5 cm reflects historical pre-trial "radical" practice and 4 cm was the wider comparator that RCTs showed was unnecessary — or misstate the in-situ margin.
B113.105.hy: In situ 0.5–1.0 cm; 2 cm is the maximum useful margin.

B113.106.stem: For melanoma in situ of the lentigo maligna type on the face, which approach is favored?

options.A: Mohs/staged excision with complete margin assessment; subclinical spread may need ≥1 cm to clear
options.B: A fixed 5 mm peripheral margin, which is reliably sufficient for facial lentigo maligna in all cases
options.C: Cryotherapy applied to the visible lesion as the first-line definitive treatment of choice for the face
options.D: Observation without treatment, on the basis that lentigo maligna essentially never progresses to invasion

B113.106.exp: Lentigo maligna (the in-situ phase of lentigo maligna melanoma) on the face is best treated with Mohs micrographic surgery or staged ("slow Mohs") excision that provides complete circumferential peripheral and deep margin assessment (CCPDMA), because LM characteristically extends subclinically along hair follicles and adnexa well beyond its visible edge — often requiring 1 cm or more of clearance — so standard fixed-margin excision frequently leaves positive margins. Examining 100% of the margin, rather than the random sections of routine pathology, both spares uninvolved facial tissue (cosmesis) and reduces local recurrence. The principle is margin-controlled surgery for an ill-defined tumor at a tissue-sensitive site. The distractors are false: a fixed 5 mm margin commonly fails for facial LM, cryotherapy is not first-line definitive treatment, and LM is not benign — it can progress to invasive melanoma.
B113.106.hy: Facial MIS-LM → Mohs/staged excision (CCPDMA); subclinical spread may need ≥1 cm.

B113.107.stem: Topical imiquimod is sometimes used for lentigo maligna. What is its mechanism, and when is it typically used?

options.A: A Toll-like receptor (TLR)-7 agonist; used as primary therapy when surgery is unsuitable, or adjunctively
options.B: An alkylating cytotoxic chemotherapy cream applied directly to the surgical tumor bed after resection
options.C: A topical BRAF-inhibitor preparation applied to V600E-mutant lentigo maligna to shrink it before surgery
options.D: A first-line replacement for wide local excision in essentially all invasive cutaneous melanomas

B113.107.exp: Imiquimod is a topical Toll-like receptor (TLR)-7 agonist: it activates innate immunity (inducing interferon-α and other cytokines) to provoke a local antitumor immune response, which is why it has a niche in *lentigo maligna* (in-situ disease). It is used either as primary therapy when surgery is unattractive — elderly or poor surgical candidates, or large disfiguring facial lesions — or as adjuvant therapy when excision leaves narrow (≤1 mm) or transected margins with no clinically apparent residual tumor. Its role is strictly limited to in-situ disease because it cannot reliably eradicate an invasive tumor's deep component. The distractors mischaracterize it as alkylating chemotherapy, as a BRAF inhibitor (a targeted oral drug for mutant invasive disease), or as a substitute for surgical excision of invasive melanoma — none of which it is.
B113.107.hy: Imiquimod = TLR-7 agonist; LM primary (non-surgical) or adjuvant.

B113.108.stem: What is the current evidence-based status of Mohs micrographic surgery (MMS) for invasive cutaneous melanoma?

options.A: Evidence is insufficient to recommend MMS for invasive melanoma; its recognized role is mainly MIS (LM/acral)
options.B: MMS is the established, guideline-endorsed standard of care for all invasive cutaneous melanomas at any site
options.C: MMS is formally contraindicated for any melanoma whatsoever, including melanoma in situ of the head and neck
options.D: MMS has demonstrated clearly superior melanoma-specific survival in large prospective randomized trials

B113.108.exp: Mohs micrographic surgery (MMS) examines 100% of the surgical margin intraoperatively, which is ideal for tumors with ill-defined subclinical extension, and in melanoma its established role is for *in-situ* disease — lentigo maligna (chronic sun-damage type) and acral types, and at most minimally invasive T1a lentigo maligna melanoma at anatomically constrained sites — where margin-controlled excision improves local control and spares tissue. For frankly *invasive* melanoma the evidence is currently insufficient for guidelines to recommend MMS as standard, partly because of historical concerns about interpreting melanocytes on frozen sections and the lack of demonstrated survival benefit. So the principle is: MMS for selected in-situ/superficial disease, conventional wide excision (± SLNB) for invasive disease. The distractors overstate it (calling it standard for all invasive melanoma or claiming a proven melanoma-specific survival benefit) or wrongly forbid it altogether.
B113.108.hy: MMS: established for MIS (LM/acral), not yet recommended for invasive melanoma.

B113.109.stem: Adjuvant pembrolizumab is approved for which resected node-negative (stage II) melanomas?

options.A: Stage IIB (T3b/T4a N0) and stage IIC (T4b N0)
options.B: Stage IA disease (a T1a N0 thin melanoma)
options.C: All resected melanoma in-situ (stage 0) lesions
options.D: Only resected stage IV distant metastatic disease

B113.109.exp: Pembrolizumab (an anti-PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor) is approved as *adjuvant* therapy after complete resection of high-risk but still node-negative stage IIB (T3b/T4a N0) and IIC (T4b N0) melanoma, based on a phase 3 trial in which adjuvant anti-PD-1 reduced recurrence and death versus placebo. This extended checkpoint blockade — previously reserved for resected stage III and for metastatic disease — down into the highest-risk node-negative group, reflecting that thick or ulcerated primaries relapse often enough to justify systemic immunotherapy even without proven nodal spread. The teaching point is that adjuvant ICI eligibility now tracks recurrence risk, not just nodal positivity. The distractors are out of indication: stage IA and in-situ disease are far too low-risk to treat systemically, and the stage II approval is specifically for resected node-negative high-risk *primaries*, not for established stage IV (metastatic) disease, which is treated differently.
B113.109.src: Bologna Ch 113 · Adjuvant Therapy for Stage II

B113.110.stem: For which clinical scenario is sentinel lymph node biopsy NOT typically recommended?

options.A: T1a melanoma (<0.8 mm, no ulceration), with SLN positivity under 5%
options.B: T2a melanoma (>1–2 mm thick, without ulceration of the primary tumor)
options.C: T3b melanoma (>2–4 mm thick, with histologic ulceration of the primary)
options.D: T4a melanoma (>4 mm thick, without ulceration of the primary tumor)

B113.110.exp: Sentinel lymph node biopsy is offered when the chance of finding occult nodal disease is high enough to justify the procedure, and for T1a melanoma (<0.8 mm, non-ulcerated) that probability is <5% — too low to warrant SLNB routinely, since the staging yield rarely outweighs operative risk. The decision ladder runs: Tis/T1a → not typically; T1b → discuss/consider (~5–10% positivity); T2a and above → discuss and offer (>10%). This thickness-and-ulceration–driven gate is exactly why accurate Breslow microstaging on the initial excisional biopsy matters so much. The distractors name the scenarios where SLNB *is* indicated — T2a (>10% positivity) and the clearly higher-risk T3b and T4a tumors — so they are the inverse of the correct "not recommended" answer.
B113.110.hy: No routine SLNB for T1a (<0.8 mm, no ulcer; <5% positive).

B113.111.stem: Approximately what proportion of clinically node-negative patients with melanoma >1 mm thick have a positive sentinel node?
B113.111.exp: Among clinically node-negative patients with melanoma >1 mm thick, roughly 15–20% turn out to harbor microscopic (occult) nodal metastasis on sentinel-node biopsy — which is the whole reason SLNB is informative: a sizable minority of "node-negative on exam" patients are actually stage III. That positivity rate is not fixed; it climbs with increasing Breslow thickness, ulceration, high mitotic rate, and lymphovascular invasion, the same adverse features that drive T-staging. Knowing the baseline figure helps calibrate counseling and explains why thicker/ulcerated tumors are the ones offered SLNB. The distractors quote rates far above or below this 15–20% range, which would either overstate or understate the procedure's yield.
B113.111.hy: ~15–20% SLNB positivity for melanoma >1 mm, node-negative clinically.

B113.112.stem: During SLNB, which is a criterion for identifying a sentinel node intraoperatively?

options.A: A node emitting ≥10% of the hottest node's tracer counts (and/or dyed blue or palpably enlarged)
options.B: Only the single anatomically largest node in the basin, irrespective of tracer uptake or dye
options.C: Specifically any node that fails to take up either the blue dye or the radiotracer at all
options.D: All lymph nodes lying within 5 cm of the primary tumor, removed empirically en bloc

B113.112.exp: The sentinel node is the first node draining the tumor, and it is identified intraoperatively by a dual-mapping technique: preoperative lymphoscintigraphy plus intradermal blue dye and a radiotracer are injected around the primary, and the surgeon then removes any node that is dyed blue, any node that is the "hottest" by the gamma probe, any node emitting ≥10% of the counts of the hottest node, and any clinically palpable node. The ≥10% rule and the blue/hot criteria ensure that all true first-echelon nodes are captured, not just the single most prominent one, which is what makes the staging reliable. This selective harvest is the entire point — sample the nodes most likely to contain early metastasis without removing the whole basin. The distractors contradict the protocol: taking only one largest node, taking nodes that do *not* take up tracer, or empirically removing all nearby nodes (which would be a completion dissection, not a sentinel biopsy).
B113.112.hy: SLN = blue, hottest, ≥10% of hottest, or palpable.

B113.113.stem: What best describes the role of SLNB in melanoma?

options.A: A prognostic/staging procedure not shown to improve survival, but it finds occult stage III disease
options.B: A therapeutic procedure that, by removing the first draining node, independently cures regional nodal disease
options.C: A procedure that has now replaced wide local excision as the definitive treatment of the primary tumor
options.D: A staging step that is required in every melanoma, including thin melanoma in situ (stage 0) lesions

B113.113.exp: SLNB is fundamentally a *staging/prognostic* procedure, not a treatment: in the MSLT-1 trial it did not improve overall survival across the whole population (a benefit was seen only in select intermediate-thickness subgroups), so its purpose is to detect occult stage III disease rather than to cure. That information is highly valuable, however, because identifying a positive node refines prognosis and, in the modern era, qualifies the patient for adjuvant systemic therapy that improves relapse-free survival. So the chain is: SLNB stages → positive node → adjuvant ICI/targeted therapy → better recurrence-free outcomes, even though the biopsy itself is not therapeutic. The distractors overstate it — calling SLNB curative/therapeutic or a replacement for wide local excision (it complements, not replaces, WLE) — or wrongly apply it to in-situ disease, which has essentially no metastatic risk.
B113.113.hy: SLNB = prognostic, not therapeutic; enables adjuvant therapy.

B113.114.stem: Following a positive SLNB, why has completion lymph node dissection (CLND) declined?

options.A: Two RCTs (MSLT-2, DeCOG) showed no overall-survival benefit versus nodal-ultrasound surveillance
options.B: Completion dissection was actually shown in randomized trials to worsen melanoma-specific survival
options.C: Completion dissection is technically impossible to perform once the sentinel node has been removed
options.D: Nodal ultrasound surveillance now provides full pathologic non-sentinel-node data, making it redundant

B113.114.exp: Completion lymph node dissection (CLND) — removing all remaining nodes in a basin after a positive sentinel node — has been largely abandoned worldwide because two randomized trials, MSLT-2 (international) and the German DeCOG-SLT, both showed no overall-survival benefit from immediate CLND versus active observation with nodal ultrasound surveillance, while CLND adds real morbidity (notably lymphedema and wound complications). The modern standard after a positive SLN is therefore close ultrasound follow-up of the basin plus consideration of adjuvant systemic therapy, reserving dissection for nodal relapse. This is a landmark practice change driven directly by level-1 evidence. The distractors are false: the trials did not show CLND worsens melanoma-specific survival, CLND remains technically feasible, and nodal ultrasound — its key limitation — does NOT provide pathologic information on the non-sentinel nodes, it only monitors for clinically detectable recurrence.
B113.114.hy: No OS benefit for CLND (MSLT-2/DeCOG) → nodal US surveillance.
