B113.115.stem: A patient develops indurated papulonodules within 2 cm of the melanoma excision scar (satellitosis). How does this change staging?

options.A: It reclassifies the disease as stage III — prompting imaging, wide excision/possible SLNB, and adjuvant discussion
options.B: It remains stage I provided the original primary tumor was thin and had been completely excised
options.C: It is automatically stage IV distant metastatic disease regardless of how close it lies to the scar
options.D: It carries no staging implication at all as long as the original surgical margins were histologically clear

B113.115.exp: New papules or nodules appearing within ~2 cm of a melanoma scar are satellite metastases — a form of regional lymphatic (in-transit) spread — which by definition reclassify the disease as stage III (an N-category "c" lesion), not local scar-site recurrence to be ignored. The correct response is to treat it as new regional disease: restage with appropriate imaging, refer for wide excision of the deposits and consideration of nodal evaluation, and discuss adjuvant or systemic therapy, since stage III carries meaningful metastatic risk. Recognizing that lymphatic deposits are stage III — neither trivial nor automatically incurable — is the management pivot. The distractors either understage it (calling it stage I with no implications) or overstage it (labeling it automatically stage IV distant disease, which would require spread beyond the regional lymphatics).
B113.115.hy: Satellite/in-transit recurrence = stage III → restage + adjuvant discussion.

B113.116.stem: Talimogene laherparepvec (TVEC), used intralesionally for unresectable cutaneous/in-transit metastases, works by:

options.A: Being an engineered herpes virus that replicates in tumor cells and secretes GM-CSF to drive antitumor immunity
options.B: Acting as a systemically administered alkylating cytotoxic chemotherapy that crosslinks tumor-cell DNA
options.C: Combining a BRAF inhibitor with a MEK inhibitor to shut down constitutive MAPK pathway signaling
options.D: Functioning as an anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody that releases the brake on cytotoxic T lymphocytes

B113.116.exp: Talimogene laherparepvec (TVEC) is an oncolytic immunotherapy — a genetically engineered herpes simplex virus type 1 that is attenuated so it selectively replicates within tumor cells, lysing them, and is armed with the GM-CSF gene so that as the cells rupture they release GM-CSF, recruiting and activating dendritic cells and T cells to mount a systemic antitumor immune response. It is injected directly into accessible lesions, so its niche is unresectable cutaneous, subcutaneous, or in-transit melanoma deposits, and it has also been studied in combination with checkpoint inhibitors. The dual mechanism — direct lysis plus an in-situ vaccine effect — is the testable concept. The distractors describe completely different drug classes: alkylating chemotherapy, BRAF/MEK targeted inhibition, and anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibodies, none of which is an oncolytic virus.
B113.116.hy: TVEC = oncolytic engineered HSV expressing GM-CSF.
B113.116.src: Bologna Ch 113 · Skin Satellite and In-Transit Metastases

B113.117.stem: For multiple in-transit metastases confined to one limb, which regional treatment may have palliative (sometimes curative) value?

options.A: Isolated limb perfusion using melphalan, with or without added TNF
options.B: Whole-brain external-beam radiotherapy delivered over several fractions
options.C: Systemic single-agent dacarbazine chemotherapy given intravenously
options.D: Prophylactic completion lymphadenectomy of the regional nodal basin

B113.117.exp: When in-transit metastases are numerous but confined to one extremity, isolated limb perfusion (ILP) is the regional treatment of choice: the limb's circulation is surgically isolated from the systemic circulation and perfused with high-dose chemotherapy — melphalan, often with tumor necrosis factor (TNF) — so the tumor deposits receive cytotoxic concentrations that would be intolerable systemically, sparing the rest of the body. It offers durable palliation and sometimes long-term control (reported ~40% 5-year and ~30% 10-year survival in this setting). The principle is regional, high-dose, anatomically confined therapy for anatomically confined disease. The distractors are mismatched: whole-brain radiotherapy treats CNS disease, single-agent dacarbazine is weak systemic salvage chemotherapy, and a prophylactic lymphadenectomy addresses nodes, not the in-transit dermal/subcutaneous deposits in the limb.
B113.117.hy: Limb in-transit disease → isolated limb perfusion (melphalan ± TNF).
B113.117.src: Bologna Ch 113 · Skin Satellite and In-Transit Metastases

B113.118.stem: For resected stage III melanoma with a BRAF V600 mutation, which adjuvant targeted regimen is approved?

options.A: Dabrafenib plus trametinib (a BRAF inhibitor with a MEK inhibitor)
options.B: Imatinib monotherapy directed against the KIT receptor tyrosine kinase
options.C: High-dose interleukin-2 immunostimulation given as inpatient therapy
options.D: Larotrectinib, the selective TRK inhibitor used for NTRK-fusion tumors

B113.118.exp: For completely resected stage III melanoma that carries a BRAF V600 mutation, adjuvant dabrafenib plus trametinib is an approved option — dabrafenib inhibits the mutant BRAF kinase and trametinib inhibits the downstream MEK kinase, so the pair shuts down the constitutively active MAPK pathway driving these tumors, reducing recurrence. The mutation is the gatekeeper: this combination only works (and is only indicated) when a V600 BRAF mutation is present, which is why molecular testing precedes the choice between adjuvant targeted therapy and adjuvant immunotherapy. The unifying concept is matching the drug to the tumor's driver. The distractors target the wrong driver or era: imatinib is for KIT-mutant melanomas (acral/mucosal), high-dose IL-2 is older immunotherapy for unresectable disease, and larotrectinib treats the rare NTRK-fusion–positive tumors — none is the BRAF-mutant adjuvant standard.
B113.118.hy: Adjuvant dabrafenib+trametinib for resected stage III BRAF V600+.

B113.119.stem: Why are BRAF inhibitors combined with MEK inhibitors in BRAF-mutant melanoma?

options.A: The combination delays resistance (response ~10 months) and cuts the cutaneous SCCs/KAs of BRAFi monotherapy
options.B: The MEK inhibitor is required to activate the BRAF inhibitor, which is an inactive prodrug on its own
options.C: Adding a MEK inhibitor reliably eliminates all of the immune-related adverse events of the regimen
options.D: The MEK inhibitor is needed to treat the NRAS mutation that every BRAF-mutant melanoma also carries

B113.119.exp: BRAF inhibitors work spectacularly at first but tumors develop acquired resistance, usually by reactivating the MAPK pathway downstream of BRAF; adding a MEK inhibitor blocks that escape route, which both delays resistance (extending the average duration of response to roughly 10 months) and — importantly — reduces the cutaneous toxicities of BRAF-inhibitor monotherapy. Those toxicities (squamous cell carcinomas and keratoacanthomas) arise from paradoxical MAPK activation in BRAF–wild-type keratinocytes, so suppressing MEK downstream prevents that paradox. Hence BRAF + MEK combination, not BRAF alone, is standard for BRAF-mutant disease. The distractors offer false mechanisms: BRAF inhibitors are not prodrugs that require MEK to activate, the combination does not eliminate immune-related adverse events (those belong to checkpoint inhibitors), and not every BRAF-mutant tumor harbors an NRAS mutation.
B113.119.hy: BRAFi+MEKi: delays resistance (~10 mo) and cuts cuSCC/KA.

B113.120.stem: BRAF-inhibitor monotherapy induces cutaneous squamous cell carcinomas/keratoacanthomas in up to ~25% of patients because of:

options.A: Paradoxical MAPK pathway activation in wild-type-BRAF (often RAS-mutant) keratinocytes
options.B: Direct chemical mutagenesis of keratinocyte DNA by the small-molecule drug itself
options.C: Drug-induced local immunosuppression of the skin permitting keratinocyte outgrowth
options.D: Reactivation of latent oncogenic human papillomavirus within the epidermal keratinocytes

B113.120.exp: BRAF-inhibitor monotherapy induces cutaneous squamous cell carcinomas and keratoacanthomas in up to ~25% of patients by a *paradoxical* mechanism: in keratinocytes that have wild-type BRAF (and frequently a pre-existing RAS mutation), the drug actually drives, rather than blocks, the MAPK pathway — so the same inhibitor that suppresses signaling in BRAF-mutant melanoma cells stimulates proliferation in BRAF–wild-type skin cells. This explains both why the lesions appear quickly on therapy and why adding a downstream MEK inhibitor sharply reduces them (it interrupts the paradoxically activated pathway). The concept is class-defining for these drugs. The distractors offer mechanisms the chapter does not support — direct DNA mutagenesis by the drug, generalized immunosuppression, or HPV reactivation — none of which is the paradoxical-MAPK explanation.
B113.120.hy: BRAFi → paradoxical MAPK in RAS-mutant keratinocytes → cuSCC/KA (~25%).

B113.121.stem: Which statement about first-line systemic therapy for unresectable/metastatic melanoma is correct?

options.A: ICIs are generally preferred first-line regardless of BRAF status; ipilimumab is largely supplanted by anti-PD-1 agents
options.B: BRAF inhibitors are recommended first-line even for tumors that are confirmed to be BRAF-wild-type on testing
options.C: Single-agent ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) is preferred over anti-PD-1 monotherapy because it is less toxic overall
options.D: Cytotoxic chemotherapy with dacarbazine remains the standard first-line treatment of metastatic melanoma

B113.121.exp: For unresectable or metastatic melanoma, immune checkpoint inhibitors are generally the preferred first-line systemic therapy regardless of BRAF status, because they produce durable, sometimes years-long responses across mutation types — an advantage over targeted therapy, whose responses, while fast, tend to be time-limited. Within the checkpoint class, the original anti-CTLA-4 agent ipilimumab (which releases the early "priming" brake on T cells) has been largely supplanted as monotherapy by the anti-PD-1 antibodies nivolumab and pembrolizumab (which release the "effector" brake in the tumor), as anti-PD-1 is both more effective and less toxic; the two are still combined in selected high-risk patients. The teaching point is that ICIs are mutation-agnostic and first-line. The distractors are false: BRAF inhibitors require a BRAF mutation (not all-comers), single-agent ipilimumab is inferior to anti-PD-1, and cytotoxic chemotherapy is now reserved for salvage after immunotherapy and targeted options.
B113.121.hy: ICIs first-line (any BRAF status); anti-PD-1 > ipilimumab.

B113.122.stem: Approximately what proportion of cutaneous melanomas carry an activating BRAF mutation, and what fraction of BRAF mutations are V600E?

options.A: ~45% are BRAF-mutant; ~90% of BRAF mutations are V600E
options.B: ~80% are BRAF-mutant; about half of those mutations are V600E
options.C: ~15% are BRAF-mutant; only about 10% of those are V600E
options.D: ~95% are BRAF-mutant; nearly all of those are V600K, not V600E

B113.122.exp: Roughly 45% of cutaneous melanomas harbor an activating BRAF mutation and about 15% carry an NRAS mutation — both drive the same MAPK signaling pathway — and of the BRAF mutations, approximately 90% are the V600E variant (a valine-to-glutamic-acid substitution at codon 600), with V600K and others making up the remainder. These proportions are clinically load-bearing because BRAF V600 status is precisely what determines eligibility for BRAF/MEK targeted therapy, so they explain why nearly half of patients have a targetable driver. Carrying the "45% BRAF, 90% of those V600E" figures lets you reason about how many patients are candidates for dabrafenib/trametinib-type regimens. The distractors swap in wrong proportions (80%/50%; 15%/10%; or claiming 95% are V600K), which would misrepresent how common the targetable mutation is.
B113.122.hy: ~45% BRAF-mutant; ~90% of BRAF mutations are V600E.

B113.123.stem: Why is caution needed when sequencing a BRAF inhibitor shortly after an immune checkpoint inhibitor?

options.A: Severe drug hypersensitivity (DRESS) occurs in ~20% when a BRAFi follows ICI priming, versus ~5% without
options.B: The overlapping combination predictably triggers an immediate, life-threatening tumor lysis syndrome
options.C: Immune checkpoint inhibitors permanently and irreversibly inactivate the BRAF inhibitor molecule
options.D: The sequence reliably cures essentially all treated patients, so no special caution is actually needed

B113.123.exp: Drug sequencing matters in melanoma because severe hypersensitivity — including DRESS (drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms, a delayed multi-organ reaction with rash, fever, eosinophilia, and hepatic/renal involvement) — occurs in about 20% of patients when a BRAF inhibitor is started soon after immune-checkpoint-inhibitor "priming," compared with only ~5% without prior ICI. The leading explanation is that checkpoint blockade leaves the immune system sensitized, so a subsequent targeted drug is more likely to trigger an exaggerated reaction. This is a practical reason to be deliberate about the order and timing of immunotherapy versus targeted therapy. The distractors invent unrelated mechanisms (tumor lysis syndrome, inactivation of the BRAF inhibitor) or wrongly claim the ICI-then-BRAFi sequence is uniformly safe or curative.

B113.124.stem: Which is an appropriate use of radiation therapy in melanoma?

options.A: Stereotactic radiosurgery for brain mets; primary RT for unresectable MIS-LM; adjuvant RT for high-risk desmoplastic tumors
options.B: Routine first-line definitive treatment of all localized cutaneous melanomas in place of surgical excision
options.C: Routine adjuvant irradiation of the nodal basin after lymphadenectomy, on the basis of a proven survival benefit
options.D: As a standard substitute for wide local excision in the management of thin (T1) cutaneous melanomas

B113.124.exp: Melanoma is relatively radioresistant, so radiation therapy is used only in specific niches rather than as primary curative treatment: stereotactic radiosurgery for brain metastases (focused high-dose treatment of discrete lesions), primary RT for unresectable melanoma in situ of the lentigo maligna type (when surgery is not feasible), and adjuvant RT for high-risk desmoplastic melanoma, particularly the neurotropic variant prone to local/perineural recurrence. The unifying logic is that RT is reserved for control where surgery is impossible or recurrence risk is locally high, not for ordinary cutaneous disease. The distractors misstate this: RT is rarely used for localized cutaneous melanoma, routine adjuvant radiation of the nodal basin improved local control but showed no survival benefit at the cost of significant toxicity (e.g. lymphedema), and RT does not substitute for the wide local excision that cures thin melanomas.
B113.124.hy: RT niches: SRS for brain mets, unresectable MIS-LM, adjuvant for desmoplastic.

B113.125.stem: Which statement about melanoma recurrence and second primaries is correct?

options.A: About 90% of recurrences occur within 5 years (greatest in the first 2); second-primary risk is ~3.5–4.5%
options.B: Recurrences are spread evenly across the first 20 years of follow-up, showing no early post-diagnosis peak
options.C: Late recurrence occurring beyond 10–15 years from diagnosis is by far the most common pattern of relapse
options.D: The lifetime risk of developing a second primary cutaneous melanoma exceeds 50% in most treated patients

B113.125.exp: Melanoma recurrence is heavily front-loaded — roughly 90% of recurrences happen within the first 5 years and the greatest density is in the first 2 — which is exactly why surveillance schedules are most intensive early and then taper. Separately, a melanoma survivor's lifetime risk of developing a *second primary* melanoma is about 3.5–4.5%, well above the general-population baseline, and that risk persists indefinitely, justifying continued (lifelong) skin surveillance even after the recurrence window narrows. The two facts together explain the surveillance design: frequent early visits to catch relapse, lifelong periodic skin checks to catch new primaries. The distractors are false: recurrences are not spread evenly over time, late recurrence beyond 10–15 years is uncommon rather than the dominant pattern, and the second-primary risk is single-digit, not greater than 50%.
B113.125.hy: 90% of recurrences in first 5 yr; second-primary risk ~3.5–4.5%.

B113.126.stem: A typical recommended dermatologic follow-up schedule after melanoma diagnosis is:

options.A: 1–4 times/year for 2 years, then every 6–12 months through year 5, then at least annually
options.B: A single comprehensive skin examination visit at the 5-year mark, followed by discharge from care
options.C: Weekly dermatologic visits continued indefinitely for the remainder of the patient's lifetime
options.D: Whole-body imaging every month for life, regardless of the original stage at diagnosis

B113.126.exp: Melanoma follow-up cadence is deliberately front-loaded to mirror the recurrence risk: most experts recommend dermatologic visits 1–4 times per year for the first 2 years (frequency scaled to stage/risk), then every 6–12 months through year 5, then at least annually for life. The early intensity targets the window when ~90% of recurrences occur, while the indefinite annual checks address the ongoing elevated risk of a second primary melanoma. Visits combine total-body skin examination and regional nodal palpation (with stage-directed imaging for higher-stage patients), not blanket imaging for everyone. The distractors are implausible extremes — a single visit at 5 years (misses the high-risk early period), weekly visits (no rationale), or monthly lifelong imaging (excess radiation/cost without benefit).
B113.126.hy: Follow-up: 1–4×/yr ×2 yr → q6–12 mo to year 5 → annually.

B115.1.stem: Neurofibromas are benign nerve-sheath tumors composed of a proliferation of which mix of cells?

options.A: Schwann cells, perineurial cells, fibroblasts, and mast cells, plus residual axons
options.B: A pure clonal proliferation of encapsulated Schwann cells forming Verocay bodies
options.C: Dendritic melanocytes set within a fibrotic, heavily pigmented dermal stroma
options.D: Storiform CD34-positive fibrohistiocytic spindle cells infiltrating the fat

B115.1.exp: A neurofibroma is a benign nerve-sheath tumor formed by proliferation of the entire "neuromesenchyme" — Schwann cells, perineurial cells, endoneurial fibroblasts, and mast cells — together with residual interspersed axons. That mixed-lineage composition is the defining contrast with a schwannoma, which is a *pure* Schwann-cell tumor (encapsulated, with Antoni A/B areas and Verocay bodies). Because axons traverse the tumor, the axon-to-Schwann-cell ratio is lower than the normal 1:1, and the mast-cell component is biologically important (it drives growth in NF1 — see B115.3). Solitary cutaneous neurofibromas are common and have no age or sex preference; finding *multiple* neurofibromas should raise the possibility of a neurofibromatosis. Each distractor is a different real dermal tumor: a pigmented dendritic-melanocyte proliferation in fibrotic stroma is a blue nevus, and a storiform CD34-positive spindle-cell tumor that infiltrates fat is dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans.
B115.1.hy: Neurofibroma = mixed neuromesenchyme (Schwann + perineurial + fibroblast + mast cells, plus axons); schwannoma = pure Schwann cells.

B115.2.stem: A patient has a soft, pendulous "bag of worms" mass. This lesion (a plexiform neurofibroma) is:

options.A: Pathognomonic of neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1)
options.B: A characteristic skin finding of tuberous sclerosis complex
options.C: Diagnostic of NF2, which features vestibular schwannomas
options.D: A hallmark of Carney complex with its cardiac myxomas

B115.2.exp: A plexiform neurofibroma is a tortuous, rope-like proliferation of neurofibromatous tissue along a nerve and its branches, producing a soft, pendulous "bag of worms" mass; it is considered congenital and is *pathognomonic of neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1)*. Recognizing one is clinically important because it should trigger a search for the other NF1 features — Lisch nodules (iris hamartomas), axillary/inguinal freckling, and café-au-lait macules — and because plexiform neurofibromas are the lesions at risk of malignant transformation to an MPNST (B115.4). Extensive involvement of a limb can cause "elephantiasis neuromatosa." The distractors are different genodermatoses with their own hallmarks: tuberous sclerosis (angiofibromas, shagreen patch, ash-leaf macules), NF2 (bilateral vestibular schwannomas), and Carney complex (lentigines plus cardiac myxomas).
B115.2.hy: Plexiform neurofibroma ("bag of worms") = pathognomonic of NF1, and the precursor at risk for MPNST.

B115.3.stem: Which statement about the pathogenesis of a cutaneous neurofibroma is correct?

options.A: Only a Schwann-cell subset is neoplastic (biallelic NF1 loss), aided by NF1+/− mast cells
options.B: Every cell in the tumor carries the same somatic BRAF V600E activating point mutation
options.C: It is a monoclonal proliferation driven by an inherited germline RET receptor mutation
options.D: Its growth depends on a GNAQ-mutant melanocytic clone that expands within the dermis

B115.3.exp: The NF1 gene encodes neurofibromin, a RAS-GAP tumor suppressor that switches off RAS signaling; in a neurofibroma only a *subpopulation* of Schwann cells is truly neoplastic, carrying clonal biallelic NF1 inactivation (NF1−/−, "nullizygosity"). Crucially, that loss is necessary but not sufficient — tumor formation also requires a permissive NF1-haploinsufficient (NF1+/−) microenvironment, classically the NF1+/− mast cells, which is why neurofibromas are part-neoplasm, part-reactive. (Sporadic solitary neurofibromas often retain neurofibromin, so their pathogenesis is less clear.) The distractors assign drivers of other tumors: BRAF V600E drives melanoma and melanocytic nevi, a germline RET mutation drives MEN2 (medullary thyroid carcinoma + pheochromocytoma), and GNAQ/GNA11 drive uveal melanoma and blue nevi.
B115.3.hy: Neurofibroma = biallelic NF1 (neurofibromin/RAS-GAP) loss in a Schwann subpopulation + a promoting NF1+/− mast-cell microenvironment.

B115.4.stem: By what route does a malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor (MPNST) most characteristically arise from neurofibroma?

options.A: From a plexiform neurofibroma via deletion of the CDKN2A/CDKN2B locus
options.B: From a solitary cutaneous neurofibroma via acquired BRAF V600E mutation
options.C: From a diffuse neurofibroma via amplification of the MDM2 oncogene
options.D: From an intraneural neurofibroma via a GNAQ/GNA11 activating mutation

B115.4.exp: A malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor (MPNST) — an aggressive S100/SOX10-variable spindle-cell sarcoma with a "herringbone" fascicular pattern, marked atypia, abnormal mitoses, and necrosis — most characteristically arises from a *pre-existing plexiform neurofibroma* through deletion of the CDKN2A/CDKN2B locus (loss of the p16/p14ARF and p15 cell-cycle brakes). This is why plexiform neurofibromas (and NF1 patients) warrant monitoring: rapid growth or new pain in a long-standing plexiform lesion suggests transformation. High-grade MPNSTs also frequently lose PRC2 activity (EED/SUZ12), reflected as loss of H3K27me3 staining — a useful diagnostic clue. The distractors misassign the route or driver: solitary and diffuse neurofibromas are not the usual precursors, and BRAF V600E, MDM2 amplification, and GNAQ/GNA11 are drivers of other tumors (melanoma/nevi, sarcomas/liposarcoma, and uveal melanoma/blue nevi, respectively).
B115.4.hy: Plexiform neurofibroma → MPNST via CDKN2A/CDKN2B deletion (high-grade tumors also lose H3K27me3).

B115.5.stem: Within the spectrum of malignant change in neurofibroma, the intermediate step "ANNUBP" denotes:

options.A: Atypical neurofibromatous neoplasm of uncertain biologic potential — atypia, ↑cellularity, ↑mitoses
options.B: A wholly benign "ancient" neurofibroma whose scattered nuclear atypia carries no clinical concern
options.C: A frankly malignant high-grade sarcoma with herringbone fascicles and loss of H3K27me3
options.D: A hybrid neurofibroma/schwannoma tumor showing nuclear palisading and Verocay bodies

B115.5.exp: ANNUBP — "atypical neurofibromatous neoplasm of uncertain biologic potential" — is the recognized *intermediate* lesion on the pathway from benign neurofibroma to MPNST, defined by a variable combination of nuclear atypia, increased cellularity, and increased mitotic activity. The concept matters clinically because it separates worrisome change from harmless change: cytologic atypia *alone* in a neurofibroma is degenerative and NOT concerning (so-called "ancient neurofibroma," analogous to ancient change in other benign tumors), whereas atypia *plus* hypercellularity *plus* mitoses (ANNUBP) signals a lesion that may progress and warrants closer management. The distractors are the neighbours ANNUBP sits between: the frank high-grade sarcoma is MPNST (herringbone fascicles, H3K27me3 loss), and nuclear palisading with Verocay bodies is a hybrid neurofibroma/schwannoma.
B115.5.hy: ANNUBP = atypia + ↑cellularity + ↑mitoses (pre-malignant); atypia alone = benign "ancient" neurofibroma.

B115.6.stem: On histology, which feature is characteristic of a localized cutaneous neurofibroma?

options.A: An unencapsulated dermal spindle-cell proliferation under a grenz zone, S100-positive and mast-cell-rich
options.B: A well-encapsulated tumor with Antoni A/B areas and prominent Verocay-body nuclear palisading
options.C: Sheets of small round blue cells with abundant mitoses and perinuclear dot-like CK20 staining
options.D: Nests of large polygonal cells with granular eosinophilic cytoplasm and pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia

B115.6.exp: The localized cutaneous neurofibroma — the common variant — is an *unencapsulated* dermal proliferation that infiltrates surrounding tissue (it lacks a capsule even when grossly circumscribed), characteristically separated from the overlying epidermis by an uninvolved "grenz zone." It is built of a fine fibrillary lattice of haphazardly arranged slender, wavy/buckled spindle cells with a variably myxoid-to-fibrotic stroma, is mast-cell-rich, expresses S100 (Schwannian marker), and shows rare or absent mitoses. The "no capsule + grenz zone + slender S100+ cells + mast cells" pattern is what separates it from its mimics. The distractors are those mimics, each with its own histology: an *encapsulated* tumor with Antoni A/B areas and Verocay-body palisading is a schwannoma; sheets of small round blue cells with perinuclear-dot CK20 are Merkel cell carcinoma; and nests of large cells with granular eosinophilic cytoplasm under pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia are a granular cell tumor.
B115.6.hy: Neurofibroma histology = unencapsulated, grenz zone, slender S100+ spindle cells, mast-cell-rich, scant mitoses.

B115.7.stem: The clinical differential diagnosis of a solitary cutaneous neurofibroma most classically includes:

options.A: Intradermal melanocytic nevus, schwannoma, soft fibroma, fibrolipoma
options.B: Basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, cutaneous horn, sebaceous hyperplasia
options.C: Pyogenic granuloma, cherry hemangioma, glomus tumor, angiokeratoma
options.D: Dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans, atypical fibroxanthoma, keloid, scar

B115.7.exp: A solitary cutaneous neurofibroma usually presents as a soft, skin-colored, dome-shaped or polypoid papulonodule that often "buttonholes" (invaginates) on pressure, so its clinical differential is the set of other soft, skin-colored dermal/polypoid lesions: intradermal melanocytic nevus, schwannoma, soft fibroma (skin tag/acrochordon), and fibrolipoma. Knowing this differential matters because it is resolved by simple excision with histology (the treatment for a symptomatic or uncertain lesion). The distractors are coherent differentials for *other* presentations: scaly/pearly keratinocyte lesions (BCC, actinic keratosis, cutaneous horn), red vascular papules (pyogenic granuloma, cherry hemangioma, glomus tumor), and firm fibrohistiocytic/scar lesions (dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans, atypical fibroxanthoma, keloid).
B115.7.hy: Solitary NF clinical DDx = intradermal nevus, schwannoma, soft fibroma, fibrolipoma; treat by simple excision.

B115.8.stem: Merkel cell carcinoma is best characterized as:

options.A: An aggressive primary neuroendocrine carcinoma of skin (Toker's trabecular carcinoma)
options.B: An indolent low-grade adnexal tumor of eccrine origin with an excellent overall prognosis
options.C: A benign neuroendocrine hamartoma derived directly from epidermal touch-sensing Merkel cells
options.D: A locally aggressive but essentially non-metastasizing keratinocytic carcinoma of the elderly

B115.8.exp: Merkel cell carcinoma is an aggressive primary neuroendocrine carcinoma of the skin — originally named "trabecular carcinoma" by Toker — that shares features with other neuroendocrine tumors and metastasizes readily (≈40% of patients). The distractors invert its biology: it is neither indolent/eccrine, nor a benign hamartoma (its cell of origin is actually unclear, not the normal Merkel cell), nor a non-metastasizing keratinocytic carcinoma.
B115.8.hy: MCC = aggressive neuroendocrine carcinoma; Toker's "trabecular carcinoma."

B115.9.stem: Which epidemiologic statement about Merkel cell carcinoma is correct?

options.A: It is far more common with age (≈10,000× more likely at 80 than at 20 years), with a male predominance
options.B: It occurs predominantly in young women, with incidence falling steadily as the population ages
options.C: It affects the sexes equally and shows no relationship between patient age and tumor incidence
options.D: It is most frequent in darkly pigmented skin and is unrelated to ultraviolet sun exposure

B115.9.exp: MCC is a tumor of the elderly: incidence rises strikingly with age — an 80-year-old is roughly 10,000× more likely to develop it than a 20-year-old — with a male predominance (M:F ≈ 1.5:1), and its overall incidence is climbing as populations age. The steep age dependence reflects the two forces that drive MCC: lifetime cumulative UV damage and the decline of immune surveillance (immunosenescence), both of which accumulate with age. This is also why the tumor is rare but important — early recognition is critical given its aggressive behavior. The distractors invert the epidemiology: it is not a young-woman tumor, it is strongly age-related (not age-independent), and it arises in lightly pigmented sun-exposed skin rather than dark skin unrelated to UV.
B115.9.hy: MCC: ↑ steeply with age (≈10,000× at 80 vs 20), male predominance, rising with the aging population.
B115.9.src: Bologna Ch 115 · Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Epidemiology

B115.10.stem: Which set of risk factors is most associated with Merkel cell carcinoma?

options.A: Lightly pigmented skin, extensive sun exposure, chronic immunosuppression
options.B: Darkly pigmented skin, chronic arsenic ingestion, prior ionizing radiotherapy
options.C: Human papillomavirus infection, heavy smoking, chronic limb lymphedema
options.D: Chronic scarring, deep burns, long-standing venous stasis leg ulcers

B115.10.exp: The three major MCC risk factors are lightly pigmented skin, extensive sun (UV) exposure, and chronic immunosuppression — the last including solid-organ transplant recipients and patients with hematologic malignancies (e.g. CLL). These map onto the disease biology: UV both damages DNA (driving virus-negative, UV-signature tumors) and locally immunosuppresses, while systemic immunosuppression lets MCPyV-driven clones escape immune control — which is also why MCC responds so well to checkpoint immunotherapy. The distractors are risk factors for *other* skin cancers: arsenic ingestion, prior ionizing radiotherapy, and chronic scars/burns/stasis ulcers predispose to keratinocyte (especially squamous) carcinomas, and HPV/smoking/lymphedema belong to other tumors.
B115.10.hy: MCC risk = lightly pigmented skin + UV exposure + immunosuppression (transplant, CLL).
B115.10.src: Bologna Ch 115 · Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Epidemiology

B115.11.stem: In the United States, what fraction of Merkel cell carcinomas are driven by Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCPyV), and what characterizes the rest?

options.A: ~80% are MCPyV-driven; the other ~20% arise in sun-damaged skin with UV-signature mutations
options.B: ~20% are MCPyV-driven; the other ~80% arise from inherited CDKN2A germline mutations
options.C: ~50% are MCPyV-driven; the other ~50% are caused by human papillomavirus integration
options.D: Essentially 100% are MCPyV-driven; UV exposure plays no role in any geographic region

B115.11.exp: MCC has two distinct etiologies. In the US about 80% are driven by Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCPyV) — a virus that is ubiquitous on normal skin but very rarely integrates clonally to cause cancer — while the other ~20% are virus-negative, arising in chronically sun-damaged skin and carrying very high UV-signature mutation burdens. The split is geographic, tracking UV exposure and skin pigmentation: only ~40% are virus-driven in high-UV Australia (most are UV-driven there), versus ~90% in more pigmented Asian populations. This dichotomy is clinically meaningful — virus-negative (UV-driven) tumors tend to carry a somewhat poorer prognosis. The distractors reverse the fractions, invoke a CDKN2A germline syndrome, or wrongly credit HPV (the virus of cervical/oropharyngeal cancer, not MCC).
B115.11.hy: US MCC ≈ 80% MCPyV-driven / 20% UV-signature; Australia ~40% viral, Asia ~90% viral.
B115.11.src: Bologna Ch 115 · Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Pathogenesis

B115.12.stem: How does MCPyV cause Merkel cell carcinoma at the molecular level?

options.A: Clonal viral integration plus a truncating mutation inactivating the large T-antigen helicase
options.B: Episomal viral persistence that silences the host CDKN2A locus by promoter hypermethylation
options.C: Viral insertion that directly activates a BRAF V600E mutation within the infected Merkel cell
options.D: An E6/E7-like protein that degrades both p53 and Rb without any genomic viral integration

B115.12.exp: Two events are needed for MCPyV to cause MCC: (1) a clonal integration event splices the virus into a host chromosome, and (2) a mutation truncates the large T-antigen so that its helicase (DNA-replication) domain is inactivated. The truncation is essential because an *intact* helicase would fire viral DNA replication at the integration site and kill the cell — so only viruses that have lost helicase activity (but retained the Rb-binding oncogenic portion of large T and the small T-antigen) can transform. Critically, the tumor stays "addicted" to ongoing viral oncoprotein expression — silencing the viral proteins halts proliferation — which is the rationale behind targeting these tumor antigens immunologically. The distractors are other oncogenic mechanisms: CDKN2A promoter silencing, BRAF V600E activation, and the HPV E6/E7 pathway (which degrades p53 and Rb without genomic integration) are not how MCPyV works.
B115.12.hy: MCPyV: clonal integration + truncating large-T mutation (dead helicase) → oncoprotein-dependent tumor.
B115.12.src: Bologna Ch 115 · Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Pathogenesis

B115.13.stem: Merkel cell carcinoma typically presents clinically as:

options.A: A pink-red to violaceous, firm, rapidly growing solitary nodule, often on the head and neck
options.B: A slowly enlarging pearly papule with rolled borders and arborizing telangiectasias
options.C: A chronic scaly erythematous plaque with overlying adherent hyperkeratotic scale
options.D: A pigmented macule with an asymmetric irregular border and multiple shades of brown

B115.13.exp: MCC classically presents as a pink-red to violaceous, firm, dome-shaped, *rapidly growing* solitary nodule (it may ulcerate), favoring sun-exposed head and neck skin, then the extremities and buttocks. Its look is nonspecific — easily mistaken for a cyst, a vascular lesion, or a benign nodule — so diagnosis is frequently delayed; at presentation roughly 65% have local disease, 26% nodal, and 8% distant. The clinical lesson is a low threshold to biopsy a rapidly growing, painless, firm reddish nodule in an older, sun-damaged or immunosuppressed patient. The distractors are other tumors with different morphology: a pearly papule with arborizing telangiectasias is BCC, an adherent scaly plaque is SCC/Bowen disease, and an asymmetric, multi-colored pigmented macule is melanoma.
B115.13.hy: MCC = rapidly growing pink-red/violaceous firm nodule, head/neck; 65% present localized.
B115.13.src: Bologna Ch 115 · Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Clinical Features

B115.14.stem: On histopathology, Merkel cell carcinoma is composed of:

options.A: Monotonous small round-to-ovoid blue cells with scant cytoplasm, abundant mitoses, and necrosis
options.B: Nests of atypical keratinocytes with intercellular bridges and keratin pearl formation
options.C: Palisaded basaloid cells with peripheral clefting (retraction) from a mucinous stroma
options.D: Sheets of large epithelioid melanocytes with prominent nucleoli and pagetoid scatter

B115.14.exp: MCC is a "small round blue cell" tumor: a dermal (often deeply infiltrating) proliferation of monotonous small, round-to-ovoid basaloid cells about 2–3× the size of mature lymphocytes, with scant cytoplasm, finely dispersed ("salt-and-pepper" neuroendocrine) chromatin, inconspicuous nucleoli, abundant mitoses, and extensive single-cell and zonal necrosis. A sheet-like growth pattern is most common (followed by nested and the original "trabecular" patterns), and characteristic crush/drying artifact plus frequent intralymphatic tumor are typical clues. Because the cells are small and bland, the differential is the whole small-round-blue-cell family — hence the central role of immunohistochemistry (B115.15–16). The distractors are other tumors' histology: keratinocytes with intercellular bridges and keratin pearls are SCC; peripheral palisading with stromal retraction is BCC; and large epithelioid cells with prominent nucleoli and pagetoid scatter are melanoma.
B115.14.hy: MCC = monotonous small round blue cells, scant cytoplasm, brisk mitoses, necrosis, sheet-like growth.
B115.14.src: Bologna Ch 115 · Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Pathology

B115.15.stem: Which immunostaining pattern is considered pathognomonic of Merkel cell carcinoma?

options.A: A perinuclear dot-like pattern of cytokeratin 20 (CK20)
options.B: Diffuse strong nuclear staining for thyroid transcription factor 1
options.C: Strong cytoplasmic and nuclear positivity for the S100 protein
options.D: Membranous staining for CD31 and ERG endothelial markers

B115.15.exp: The pathognomonic immunostain of MCC is a perinuclear *dot-like* pattern of low-molecular-weight cytokeratins (classically CK20, also CK5/6, CK7), which corresponds ultrastructurally to paranuclear whorls of intermediate filaments — a paranuclear keratin "dot" you can recognize on the slide. MCC also expresses neuroendocrine markers (chromogranin, synaptophysin, neuron-specific enolase) reflecting its neuroendocrine nature, and CK20 positivity is additionally useful for spotting tumor at margins and detecting micrometastases. The distractors are markers that are wrong for MCC: TTF-1 is characteristically NEGATIVE (it is positive in small cell lung carcinoma — the key mimic), S100 is characteristically NEGATIVE (positive in melanoma), and CD31/ERG mark endothelial/vascular tumors.
B115.15.hy: MCC = perinuclear-dot CK20 (pathognomonic) + neuroendocrine markers; S100 and TTF-1 negative.
B115.15.src: Bologna Ch 115 · Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Pathology

B115.16.stem: A small-round-blue-cell dermal tumor is TTF-1–negative and S100–negative but CK20-positive. How does this help?

options.A: TTF-1 negativity argues against metastatic small cell lung carcinoma, and S100 negativity against melanoma
options.B: TTF-1 negativity confirms a melanoma, whereas CK20 positivity confirms a basal cell carcinoma
options.C: S100 positivity would be expected in Merkel cell carcinoma and rules the diagnosis out here
options.D: TTF-1 positivity is the defining marker of Merkel cell carcinoma and is required for the diagnosis

B115.16.exp: Because MCC is morphologically just one of several "small round blue cell" tumors, the diagnosis is made by a *pattern of exclusion* with immunohistochemistry. TTF-1 negativity argues against a cutaneous metastasis of small cell carcinoma (pulmonary or extrapulmonary) — the single most important mimic, which is characteristically TTF-1-POSITIVE — and S100 negativity argues against melanoma; meanwhile perinuclear-dot CK20 positively supports MCC, and MCPyV large-T-antigen staining can add confirmation. This combined "rule-out the dangerous mimics, rule-in with CK20" logic is exactly how the panel is used in practice. The distractors invert the facts: S100 and TTF-1 are negative (not positive) in MCC, and CK20 does not mark basal cell carcinoma.
B115.16.hy: MCC panel: CK20+ (rules in) / TTF-1− (vs small cell lung ca) / S100− (vs melanoma).
B115.16.src: Bologna Ch 115 · Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Pathology

B115.17.stem: In AJCC-8 staging of Merkel cell carcinoma, a 3 cm primary tumor with no nodal or distant disease is which stage?

options.A: Stage IIA
options.B: Stage I
options.C: Stage IIB
options.D: Stage III

B115.17.exp: In AJCC-8 staging of MCC, a primary tumor >2 cm with no nodal or distant disease is stage IIA (a 3 cm node-negative tumor therefore falls here). The localized strata are size- and invasion-based: stage I is ≤2 cm and node-negative, stage IIA is >2 cm and node-negative, and stage IIB is upgraded by *deep structural invasion* (bone, muscle, fascia, or cartilage) regardless of size, still node-negative. Once regional nodes are involved the tumor is stage III, and distant spread is stage IV — and note that, unlike many cancers, the *number* of positive nodes is not factored into MCC staging (any nodal involvement = III).
B115.17.hy: MCC: ≤2 cm N0 = I; >2 cm N0 = IIA; deep invasion N0 = IIB; any node+ = III; distant = IV.

B115.18.stem: Which feature defines AJCC-8 stage IIB Merkel cell carcinoma?

options.A: A primary tumor invading bone, muscle, fascia, or cartilage, with negative nodes
options.B: A primary tumor ≤2 cm in greatest dimension with clinically negative regional nodes
options.C: Any-size primary tumor with pathologically positive regional lymph nodes detected
options.D: Any primary tumor with distant metastasis confirmed on imaging or by biopsy

B115.18.exp: AJCC-8 stage IIB MCC is defined by a primary tumor that invades deep structures — bone, muscle, fascia, or cartilage — with negative regional nodes. The teaching point is that it is *local depth of invasion*, not tumor size, that drives the IIA→IIB upgrade: a small tumor that invades fascia is IIB, whereas a large (>2 cm) tumor confined to skin/subcutis is IIA. The distractors are the adjacent stages: ≤2 cm node-negative is stage I, node-positive disease (any size) is stage III, and distant metastasis is stage IV.
B115.18.hy: Stage IIB = deep structural invasion (bone/muscle/fascia/cartilage), nodes negative — invasion, not size.

B115.19.stem: What are the approximate 5-year overall survival figures for local, nodal, and distant Merkel cell carcinoma (pre-immunotherapy)?

options.A: ~50% (local), ~35% (nodal), ~15% (distant)
options.B: ~90% (local), ~70% (nodal), ~45% (distant)
options.C: ~70% (local), ~55% (nodal), ~30% (distant)
options.D: ~30% (local), ~20% (nodal), ~5% (distant)

B115.19.exp: Pre-immunotherapy 5-year overall survival fell steeply with the extent of spread — roughly 50% for local, 35% for nodal, and 15% for distant disease — underscoring why early detection and accurate nodal staging matter. Primary tumor size adds further prognostic information within localized disease: tumors ≤2 cm have ~55% 5-year survival versus ~40% for those >2 cm (without fascial invasion). These historical figures predate checkpoint immunotherapy, which has improved outcomes in advanced disease. The distractors are uniformly too optimistic or too pessimistic relative to the cited numbers.
B115.19.hy: MCC 5-yr OS ≈ 50% local / 35% nodal / 15% distant; ≤2 cm ~55% vs >2 cm ~40%.
B115.19.src: Bologna Ch 115 · Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Prognosis

B115.20.stem: Regarding sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) in Merkel cell carcinoma, which is correct?

options.A: SLNB is recommended for most patients; positivity is ~35%, and even tumors ≤5 mm are positive in ~15%
options.B: SLNB is reserved for tumors over 4 cm, since smaller Merkel cell carcinomas essentially never spread
options.C: SLNB is not useful in Merkel cell carcinoma because the tumor spreads only hematogenously
options.D: SLNB positivity is under 5%, so the procedure rarely changes staging or management

B115.20.exp: Surgery (wide excision) plus sentinel lymph node biopsy is the principal initial treatment for localized MCC, and SLNB is recommended for *most* patients because it provides key staging and prognostic information: SLN positivity is ~35% at diagnosis. Critically, even small tumors ≤5 mm are SLN-positive in ~15%, so a thin primary does NOT exclude occult nodal spread — which is why SLNB is offered broadly rather than reserved for large tumors. Higher positivity is seen with older age, immunosuppression, trunk location, and larger size; surgery is often followed by radiotherapy to the primary site (± nodal basin). The distractors are false: SLNB is not reserved for >4 cm tumors, MCC spreads lymphatically (not only hematogenously), and positivity is far above 5%.
B115.20.hy: MCC: excision + SLNB for most; ~35% positive; even ≤5 mm ~15% → don't skip it.
B115.20.src: Bologna Ch 115 · Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Treatment

B115.21.stem: What is the current first-line systemic therapy for advanced Merkel cell carcinoma?

options.A: Anti-PD-1/PD-L1 immune checkpoint inhibitors (avelumab, pembrolizumab, retifanlimab)
options.B: Combination cytotoxic chemotherapy, which produces durable long-term remissions
options.C: BRAF plus MEK inhibitor targeted therapy directed at the V600E mutation
options.D: Imatinib directed against activating KIT receptor tyrosine kinase mutations

B115.21.exp: First-line systemic therapy for advanced MCC is anti-PD-1/PD-L1 immune checkpoint blockade — avelumab (anti-PD-L1), pembrolizumab, and retifanlimab (anti-PD-1) — which significantly improved progression-free and overall survival over historical chemotherapy and produced durable responses. This works because MCC is highly immunogenic: it displays either viral (MCPyV) antigens or abundant UV-induced neoantigens, making it an ideal checkpoint-inhibitor target. The caveat is that primary or acquired resistance still affects >50% of treated patients. The distractors are wrong: cytotoxic chemotherapy gives high but non-durable responses (now reserved as later-line), and BRAF/MEK and KIT inhibitors target mutations that are not characteristic of MCC.
B115.21.hy: Advanced MCC: anti-PD-1/PD-L1 first-line (avelumab/pembrolizumab/retifanlimab); resistance affects >50%.
B115.21.src: Bologna Ch 115 · Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Treatment

B115.22.stem: Which statement about Merkel cell carcinoma surveillance is correct?

options.A: It recurs in up to ~40% of patients, with >80% of recurrences within the first 3 years
options.B: It rarely recurs (<5%), so follow-up beyond the initial excision is generally unnecessary
options.C: Recurrences are spread evenly over 20 years, with no early peak after treatment
options.D: Once excised with clear margins it does not recur, and imaging is never indicated

B115.22.exp: MCC recurs in up to ~40% of patients, and because more than 80% of recurrences happen within the first 3 years, surveillance is intensive and front-loaded. A useful tool is the serum antibody against the MCPyV oncoprotein: in the ~50% of patients who are seropositive, titers track tumor burden and rising titers can flag recurrence (so a baseline is checked early after treatment); the ~50% who are seronegative lack this marker and rely on imaging surveillance, and they carry a somewhat higher recurrence risk. The distractors understate the disease: it is not a <5% rarely-recurring tumor, recurrences are concentrated early (not spread evenly over 20 years), and clear surgical margins do not eliminate recurrence risk.
B115.22.hy: MCC recurs up to 40%, >80% within 3 yr → early intensive surveillance; oncoprotein antibody titers (seropositive) vs imaging (seronegative).
B115.22.src: Bologna Ch 115 · Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Treatment/Surveillance

B115.23.stem: This soft, pendulous subcutaneous mass felt rope-like on palpation. What is it?

options.A: Plexiform neurofibroma
options.B: Solitary cutaneous schwannoma
options.C: Cutaneous neuroma
options.D: Granular cell tumor

B115.23.exp: A soft, pendulous mass that feels rope-like ("bag of worms") on palpation is a plexiform neurofibroma — a tortuous proliferation of neurofibromatous tissue along a nerve and its branches — and it is pathognomonic of NF1, so it should prompt a search for Lisch nodules, axillary freckling, and café-au-lait macules and awareness of MPNST risk. The same-class distractors are other benign peripheral-nerve/soft-tissue tumors that present differently: a solitary schwannoma is a discrete skin-colored nodule, a cutaneous neuroma is a small papule, and a granular cell tumor is a firm dermal nodule.
B115.23.hy: Soft, rope-like "bag of worms" mass = plexiform neurofibroma (pathognomonic of NF1).

B115.24.stem: This H&E section shows an unencapsulated dermal proliferation of slender spindle cells separated from the epidermis by a grenz zone, in a mast-cell-rich fibrillary matrix (S100-positive). What is the diagnosis?

options.A: Neurofibroma
options.B: Schwannoma
options.C: Dermatofibroma
options.D: Leiomyoma

B115.24.exp: An unencapsulated dermal proliferation of slender spindle cells beneath an uninvolved grenz zone, set in a mast-cell-rich, S100-positive fibrillary matrix, is a neurofibroma — the "no capsule + grenz zone + slender S100+ cells + mast cells" combination is the recognition pattern. The same-class distractors differ histologically: a schwannoma is encapsulated with Antoni A (Verocay-body palisades) and Antoni B areas, a dermatofibroma is a CD34-negative/factor-XIIIa-positive fibrohistiocytic lesion with overlying epidermal hyperplasia, and a leiomyoma is composed of brightly eosinophilic smooth-muscle fascicles (desmin/SMA-positive, S100-negative).
B115.24.hy: Unencapsulated, grenz zone, slender S100+ spindle cells, mast-cell-rich = neurofibroma.

B115.25.stem: This firm, pink-red to violaceous, dome-shaped nodule grew rapidly on the sun-damaged skin of an older adult. Which diagnosis is most likely?

options.A: Merkel cell carcinoma
options.B: Pyogenic granuloma
options.C: Dermatofibroma
options.D: Seborrheic keratosis

B115.25.exp: A firm, pink-red to violaceous, rapidly growing dome-shaped nodule on the sun-damaged skin of an older adult is the classic look of Merkel cell carcinoma; the appearance is nonspecific, so the combination of *rapid growth + a red/violaceous firm nodule + an elderly, sun-damaged or immunosuppressed patient* is the cue to biopsy. The same-class distractors are other nodular lesions in the clinical differential that look or behave differently: a pyogenic granuloma is a friable, easily-bleeding red papule of younger patients, a dermatofibroma is a firm brown papule that dimples on pinching, and a seborrheic keratosis is a benign "stuck-on" warty plaque.
B115.25.hy: Rapidly growing firm violaceous nodule on sun-damaged elderly skin = MCC.
B115.25.src: Bologna Ch 115 · Fig 115.17, Merkel Cell Carcinoma

B115.26.stem: This H&E section shows a dermal infiltrate of monotonous small, round-to-ovoid basaloid cells with vesicular nuclei, scant cytoplasm, apoptotic cells, and mitoses. Which tumor is this?

options.A: Merkel cell carcinoma
options.B: Basal cell carcinoma
options.C: Eccrine poroma
options.D: Melanocytic nevus

B115.26.exp: A dermal infiltrate of monotonous small, round-to-ovoid basaloid blue cells with scant cytoplasm, finely dispersed neuroendocrine chromatin, apoptotic cells, and brisk mitoses is Merkel cell carcinoma; confirmation comes from perinuclear-dot CK20 with neuroendocrine markers, and TTF-1/S100 negativity (B115.15–16). The same-class distractors are other basaloid/blue-cell tumors distinguished by architecture and IHC: BCC shows peripheral palisading with stromal retraction (and is CK20-negative), an eccrine poroma shows cuboidal poroid cells with intratumoral ducts, and a melanocytic nevus shows maturing nests (CK20-negative, S100/SOX10-positive).
B115.26.hy: Monotonous small round blue cells + brisk mitoses/necrosis, CK20-dot+ = MCC.
B115.26.src: Bologna Ch 115 · Fig 115.18B, Merkel Cell Carcinoma
B115.26.imgcap: Small round blue cell dermal tumor

B115.27.stem: This immunohistochemical stain of a small-round-blue-cell tumor shows a characteristic perinuclear dot pattern. Which stain and tumor does this represent?

options.A: CK20 in Merkel cell carcinoma
options.B: TTF-1 in small cell lung carcinoma
options.C: S100 in malignant melanoma
options.D: CD20 in cutaneous lymphoma

B115.27.exp: A crisp perinuclear (paranuclear) *dot* of cytoplasmic staining in a small-round-blue-cell tumor is the CK20 pattern of Merkel cell carcinoma — the pathognomonic finding, reflecting paranuclear whorls of keratin intermediate filaments. The same-class distractors deliberately pair a real stain with the wrong tumor (and the wrong staining pattern): TTF-1 gives *nuclear* staining in small cell lung carcinoma (the key MCC mimic, and TTF-1 is negative in MCC), S100 gives nuclear/cytoplasmic staining in melanoma, and CD20 gives membranous staining in B-cell lymphoma — none produces a paranuclear CK20 dot.
B115.27.hy: Perinuclear keratin dot = CK20 in Merkel cell carcinoma (TTF-1 nuclear = small cell lung ca).
B115.27.src: Bologna Ch 115 · Fig 115.18C, Merkel Cell Carcinoma
